[[Zenkei Shibayama]], who provided a popular commentary on the [[Mumonkan]], was an abbot of the monastery. == Significance in Zen Buddhism == Nanzen-ji is not itself considered one of the "five great Zen temples of Kyoto"; however, it does play an important role in the "[[Five Mountain System]]" which was modified from Chinese roots. {{Nihongo|[[Tenryū-ji]]|天龍寺|''Tenryū-ji''}} is considered to be one of the so-called {{nihongo|[[Five Mountain System|Kyoto ''Gozan'']]|京都五山|''Kyōto gozan''}} or "five great Zen temples of Kyoto", along with {{nihongo|[[Shōkoku-ji]]|相国寺|''Shōkoku-ji''}}, {{nihongo|[[Kennin-ji]]|建仁寺|''Kennin-ji''}}, {{nihongo|[[Tōfuku-ji]]|東福寺|''Tōfuku-ji''}}, and {{nihongo|[[Manju-ji]]|満寿寺|''Manju-ji''}}. The head temple presiding over the ''Gozan'' in Kyoto is Nanzen-ji.{{Cite book|title = The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Zen Buddhism|last = Baroni|first = Helen Josephine|publisher = The Rosen Publishing Group|year = 2002|isbn = 9780823922406|pages = 116|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=smNM4ElP3XgC}} After the completion of [[Shōkoku-ji]] by [[Ashikaga Yoshimitsu]] in 1386, a new ranking system was created with Nanzen-ji at the top and in a class of its own. Nanzen-ji had the title of "First Temple of The Land" and played a supervising role.{{Cite book|title = Zen Buddhism: A History (Japan)|last = Dumoulin|first = Heinrich|publisher = World Wisdom, Inc.|year = 2005|isbn = 9780941532907|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=hfMkpD_Xr3sC}} {| class="wikitable" | colspan="3" align="center" | Nanzen-ji |- |  !Kyoto !Kamakura |- |First Rank |[[Tenryū-ji]] |[[Kenchō-ji]] |- |Second Rank |[[Shōkoku-ji]] |[[Engaku-ji]] |- |Third Rank |[[Kennin-ji]] |[[Jufuku-ji]] |- |Fourth Rank |[[Tōfuku-ji]] |[[Jōchi-ji]] |- |Fifth Rank |[[Manju-ji]] |[[Jōmyō-ji (Kamakura)|Jōmyō-ji]] |- |} == Notable structures == === Sanmon === [[File:Nanzenji Sanmon 1.jpg|thumb|View from the second story of the Nanzen-ji Sanmon]] The temple's Sanmon gate was originally constructed in the 13th century, destroyed in 1369 at the order of the government, and reconstructed in 1628.{{Cite book|title = Daitokuji: The Visual Cultures of a Zen Monastery|last = Levine|first = Gregory P.A.|publisher = University of Washington Press|year = 2005|isbn = 9780295985404|pages = [https://archive.org/details/daitokujivisualc0000levi/page/94 94]|url = https://archive.org/details/daitokujivisualc0000levi|url-access = registration}} The gate contains stairs to an elevated viewing area, which was the setting for a famous scene in the 1778 [[Kabuki]] play [[Sanmon Gosan no Kiri]]{{Cite book|title = Historical Dictionary of Japanese Traditional Theatre|last = Leiter|first = Samuel L.|publisher = Rowman & Littlefield|year = 2014|isbn = 9781442239111|pages = 476|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=hCkvBQAAQBAJ}} inspired by the story of the criminal [[Ishikawa Goemon]] who is said to have spoken of the beauty of the view (but who was executed prior to the construction of the current gate). === Hōjō === The hōjō (abbot's quarters) of Nanzen-ji is notable both for its gardens and its art. The garden of the hōjō is considered one of the most significant examples of [[Japanese rock garden|karesansui gardens]] (rock gardens), and was built in the 1600s by [[Kobori Masakazu|Kobori Enshu]]. The garden mirrors natural forms, and is seventy percent gravel.{{Cite book|title = Art Of Japanese Gardens|last = Kuck|first = Loraine|publisher = Routledge|year = 2006|isbn = 9781136213625|pages = 230|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=DZ9FAAAAQBAJ}} It has been designated a national Place of Scenic Beauty. The hōjō itself, also known as the Seiryo-den, was given to the temple by the [[Emperor Go-Yōzei]].{{Cite book|title = The Temples of Kyoto|last = Richie|first = Donald|publisher = Tuttle Publishing|year = 2013|isbn = 9781462908578|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=ChTRAgAAQBAJ}} It contains a variety of important screen paintings on gold backgrounds, including two of tigers by [[Kanō Tan'yū]].{{Cite book|title = Of Gardens: Selected Essays|last = Deitz|first = Paula|publisher = University of Pennsylvania Press|year = 2011|isbn = 9780812206968|pages = 301|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=H9xzdCNIXvYC}} It has been designated a National Treasure. {{Gallery |File:Nanzen-ji_Ⅱ.jpg|The Hōjō |File:Nanzen-ji Ⅲ.jpg|The Hōjō garden |File:Hojo,_Konchi-in_-_IMG_5212.JPG|Crane paintings from inside the Hōjō}} === Nanzen-ji Aqueduct === Constructed in 1890 through the temple grounds to carry irrigation water from the [[Lake Biwa Canal]]. {{Gallery |File:Nanzenji aqueduct - IIMG 5411.JPG|Nanzen-ji aqueduct}} ==In media== Parts of the 2003 film ''[[Lost in Translation (film)|Lost in Translation]]'' were filmed there.{{cite web | url=https://www.imdb.com/title/tt0335266/locations/ | title=Lost in Translation (2003) - Filming & production - IMDb | website=[[IMDb]] }}{{cite web | url=https://tokyofox.net/2022/03/15/kyoto-filming-locations-lost-in-translation-2003/ | title=Kyoto Filming Locations: Lost in Translation (2003) | date=15 March 2022 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/kyoto-voice/filming-locations-kyoto/ | title=Filming Locations in Kyoto }}{{cite web | url=https://www.focusfeatures.com/article/film-locations_japan_lost-in-translation | title=Finding Japan in Lost in Translation }} == Gallery == {{Gallery |File:Kyoto Nanzenji06n3200.jpg|Hattō |File:Nanzen-in.JPG|Nanzen-in |File:Nanzenji_temple_-_IMG_5429.JPG|Small Hōjō garden |File:Zen_Garden,_Nanzen-ji_Temple_(7005735830)_(3).jpg|Another part of the complex |File:NanzenjiMomiji.jpg|In the fall }} == See also == *[[Ishikawa Goemon]] * [[List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] 978-0-7112-3447-5}} == External links == * {{Commons category-inline|Nanzen-ji}} * [https://web.archive.org/web/20081217034816/http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/ Kyoto Prefectural Tourism Guide]: Rinzai and Obaku Zen]: [http://zen.rinnou.net/head_temples/03nanzen.html Nanzen-ji] * [http://www.sacred-destinations.com/japan/kyoto.htm Sacred Destinations]: [http://www.sacred-destinations.com/japan/kyoto-nanzenji.htm Nanzen-ji, Kyoto]; * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110220214752/http://www.buddhistravel.com/index.php?kyoto Buddhist Travel]: [https://web.archive.org/web/20160303203607/http://www.buddhistravel.com/index.php?id=120,284,0,0,1,0 Nanzen-ji] {{for|the Buddhist temple in Shanxi Province, China|Nanchan Temple}} {{Short description|Zen Buddhist Temple in Kyoto, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Nanzen-ji
南禅寺 | native_name = | image = File:Kyoto_Nanzenji01s5s4272.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = The [[sanmon]] | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 86 Nanzenji Fukuchichō, [[Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|Sakyō-ku]], [[Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|0|43.14|N|135|47|39.8|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = Nanzen-ji [[Rinzai]] | deity = [[Shaka Nyorai]] (Śākyamuni) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head Temple | website = {{url|http://www.nanzen.net/english/index.html}} | founded_by = [[Emperor Kameyama]] and [[Mukan Fumon]] | year_completed = 1909 (Reconstruction) }} {{nihongo|'''Nanzen-ji'''|南禅寺|''Nanzen-ji''}}, or '''Zuiryusan Nanzen-ji''', formerly {{nihongo|'''Zenrin-ji'''|禅林寺|''Zenrin-ji''}}, is a [[Zen]] [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] [[temple]] in [[Kyoto, Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. [[Emperor Kameyama]] established it in 1291 on the site of his previous detached palace. It is also the headquarters of the Nanzen-ji branch of [[Rinzai Zen]]. The precincts of Nanzen-ji are a [[Cultural Properties of Japan|nationally designated]] [[Monuments of Japan|Historic Site]] and the Hōjō gardens a [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/00003441 |title=南禅寺境内 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1705 |title=南禅寺方丈庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012}} The temple was destroyed in a fire in 1895 and rebuilt in 1909. ==History== Nanzen-ji was founded in the middle [[Kamakura period]] (1291, or [[Shōō (Kamakura period)|Shōō]] 4 in the [[Japanese era name|Japanese era]] system).{{Cite book|title = Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794-1869|last = Ponsonby-Fane|first = Richard|year = 1956|pages = 154|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=9aLpAAAAMAAJ}} It was destroyed by fire in 1393, 1447, and 1467, rebuilt in 1597, and expanded in the Edo era.{{Cite book|title = Kyoto: a Cultural Guide|last = Martin|first = John and Phyllis|publisher = Tuttle Publishing|year = 2013|isbn = 9781462908172|pages = 177}} A large complex, it has varied over time between nine and twelve sub-temples. [[Zenkei Shibayama]], who provided a popular commentary on the [[Mumonkan]], was an abbot of the monastery. == Significance in Zen Buddhism == Nanzen-ji is not itself considered one of the "five great Zen temples of Kyoto"; however, it does play an important role in the "[[Five Mountain System]]" which was modified from Chinese roots. {{Nihongo|[[Tenryū-ji]]|天龍寺|''Tenryū-ji''}} is considered to be one of the so-called {{nihongo|[[Five Mountain System|Kyoto ''Gozan'']]|京都五山|''Kyōto gozan''}} or "five great Zen temples of Kyoto", along with {{nihongo|[[Shōkoku-ji]]|相国寺|''Shōkoku-ji''}}, {{nihongo|[[Kennin-ji]]|建仁寺|''Kennin-ji''}}, {{nihongo|[[Tōfuku-ji]]|東福寺|''Tōfuku-ji''}}, and {{nihongo|[[Manju-ji]]|満寿寺|''Manju-ji''}}. The head temple presiding over the ''Gozan'' in Kyoto is Nanzen-ji.{{Cite book|title = The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Zen Buddhism|last = Baroni|first = Helen Josephine|publisher = The Rosen Publishing Group|year = 2002|isbn = 9780823922406|pages = 116|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=smNM4ElP3XgC}} After the completion of [[Shōkoku-ji]] by [[Ashikaga Yoshimitsu]] in 1386, a new ranking system was created with Nanzen-ji at the top and in a class of its own. Nanzen-ji had the title of "First Temple of The Land" and played a supervising role.{{Cite book|title = Zen Buddhism: A History (Japan)|last = Dumoulin|first = Heinrich|publisher = World Wisdom, Inc.|year = 2005|isbn = 9780941532907|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=hfMkpD_Xr3sC}} {| class="wikitable" | colspan="3" align="center" | Nanzen-ji |- |  !Kyoto !Kamakura |- |First Rank |[[Tenryū-ji]] |[[Kenchō-ji]] |- |Second Rank |[[Shōkoku-ji]] |[[Engaku-ji]] |- |Third Rank |[[Kennin-ji]] |[[Jufuku-ji]] |- |Fourth Rank |[[Tōfuku-ji]] |[[Jōchi-ji]] |- |Fifth Rank |[[Manju-ji]] |[[Jōmyō-ji (Kamakura)|Jōmyō-ji]] |- |} == Notable structures == === Sanmon === [[File:Nanzenji Sanmon 1.jpg|thumb|View from the second story of the Nanzen-ji Sanmon]] The temple's Sanmon gate was originally constructed in the 13th century, destroyed in 1369 at the order of the government, and reconstructed in 1628.{{Cite book|title = Daitokuji: The Visual Cultures of a Zen Monastery|last = Levine|first = Gregory P.A.|publisher = University of Washington Press|year = 2005|isbn = 9780295985404|pages = [https://archive.org/details/daitokujivisualc0000levi/page/94 94]|url = https://archive.org/details/daitokujivisualc0000levi|url-access = registration}} The gate contains stairs to an elevated viewing area, which was the setting for a famous scene in the 1778 [[Kabuki]] play [[Sanmon Gosan no Kiri]]{{Cite book|title = Historical Dictionary of Japanese Traditional Theatre|last = Leiter|first = Samuel L.|publisher = Rowman & Littlefield|year = 2014|isbn = 9781442239111|pages = 476|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=hCkvBQAAQBAJ}} inspired by the story of the criminal [[Ishikawa Goemon]] who is said to have spoken of the beauty of the view (but who was executed prior to the construction of the current gate). === Hōjō === The hōjō (abbot's quarters) of Nanzen-ji is notable both for its gardens and its art. The garden of the hōjō is considered one of the most significant examples of [[Japanese rock garden|karesansui gardens]] (rock gardens), and was built in the 1600s by [[Kobori Masakazu|Kobori Enshu]]. The garden mirrors natural forms, and is seventy percent gravel.{{Cite book|title = Art Of Japanese Gardens|last = Kuck|first = Loraine|publisher = Routledge|year = 2006|isbn = 9781136213625|pages = 230|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=DZ9FAAAAQBAJ}} It has been designated a national Place of Scenic Beauty. The hōjō itself, also known as the Seiryo-den, was given to the temple by the [[Emperor Go-Yōzei]].{{Cite book|title = The Temples of Kyoto|last = Richie|first = Donald|publisher = Tuttle Publishing|year = 2013|isbn = 9781462908578|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=ChTRAgAAQBAJ}} It contains a variety of important screen paintings on gold backgrounds, including two of tigers by [[Kanō Tan'yū]].{{Cite book|title = Of Gardens: Selected Essays|last = Deitz|first = Paula|publisher = University of Pennsylvania Press|year = 2011|isbn = 9780812206968|pages = 301|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=H9xzdCNIXvYC}} It has been designated a National Treasure. {{Gallery |File:Nanzen-ji_Ⅱ.jpg|The Hōjō |File:Nanzen-ji Ⅲ.jpg|The Hōjō garden |File:Hojo,_Konchi-in_-_IMG_5212.JPG|Crane paintings from inside the Hōjō}} === Nanzen-ji Aqueduct === Constructed in 1890 through the temple grounds to carry irrigation water from the [[Lake Biwa Canal]]. {{Gallery |File:Nanzenji aqueduct - IIMG 5411.JPG|Nanzen-ji aqueduct}} ==In media== Parts of the 2003 film ''[[Lost in Translation (film)|Lost in Translation]]'' were filmed there.{{cite web | url=https://www.imdb.com/title/tt0335266/locations/ | title=Lost in Translation (2003) - Filming & production - IMDb | website=[[IMDb]] }}{{cite web | url=https://tokyofox.net/2022/03/15/kyoto-filming-locations-lost-in-translation-2003/ | title=Kyoto Filming Locations: Lost in Translation (2003) | date=15 March 2022 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/kyoto-voice/filming-locations-kyoto/ | title=Filming Locations in Kyoto }}{{cite web | url=https://www.focusfeatures.com/article/film-locations_japan_lost-in-translation | title=Finding Japan in Lost in Translation }} == Gallery == {{Gallery |File:Kyoto Nanzenji06n3200.jpg|Hattō |File:Nanzen-in.JPG|Nanzen-in |File:Nanzenji_temple_-_IMG_5429.JPG|Small Hōjō garden |File:Zen_Garden,_Nanzen-ji_Temple_(7005735830)_(3).jpg|Another part of the complex |File:NanzenjiMomiji.jpg|In the fall }} == See also == *[[Ishikawa Goemon]] * [[List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] ==References== {{Reflist|2}} ==Further reading== *{{cite book |last= Kawaguchi |first= Yoko |title= Japanese Zen Gardens |location= London |publisher= Francis Lincoln |date= 2014 |isbn= 978-0-7112-3447-5}} == External links == * {{Commons category-inline|Nanzen-ji}} * [https://web.archive.org/web/20081217034816/http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/ Kyoto Prefectural Tourism Guide]: [https://web.archive.org/web/20081221164657/http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/temples/m_nanzenji/ Nanzen-ji] * [http://zen.rinnou.net/index.html Joint Council for Japanese Rinzai and Obaku Zen]: [http://zen.rinnou.net/head_temples/03nanzen.html Nanzen-ji] * [http://www.sacred-destinations.com/japan/kyoto.htm Sacred Destinations]: [http://www.sacred-destinations.com/japan/kyoto-nanzenji.htm Nanzen-ji, Kyoto]; * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110220214752/http://www.buddhistravel.com/index.php?kyoto Buddhist Travel]: [https://web.archive.org/web/20160303203607/http://www.buddhistravel.com/index.php?id=120,284,0,0,1,0 Nanzen-ji] The hōjō itself, also known as the Seiryo-den, was given to the temple by the [[Emperor Go-Yōzei]].{{Cite book|title = The Temples of Kyoto|last = Richie|first = Donald|publisher = Tuttle Publishing|year = 2013|isbn = 9781462908578|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=ChTRAgAAQBAJ}} It contains a variety of important screen paintings on gold backgrounds, including two of tigers by [[Kanō Tan'yū]].{{Cite book|title = Of Gardens: Selected Essays|last = Deitz|first = Paula|publisher = University of Pennsylvania Press|year = 2011|isbn = 9780812206968|pages = 301|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=H9xzdCNIXvYC}} It has been designated a National Treasure. {{Gallery |File:Nanzen-ji_Ⅱ.jpg|The Hōjō |File:Nanzen-ji Ⅲ.jpg|The Hōjō garden |File:Hojo,_Konchi-in_-_IMG_5212.JPG|Crane paintings from inside the Hōjō}} === Nanzen-ji Aqueduct === Constructed in 1890 through the temple grounds to carry irrigation water from the [[Lake Biwa Canal]]. {{Gallery |File:Nanzenji aqueduct - IIMG 5411.JPG|Nanzen-ji aqueduct}} ==In media== Parts of the 2003 film ''[[Lost in Translation (film)|Lost in Translation]]'' were filmed there.{{cite web | url=https://www.imdb.com/title/tt0335266/locations/ | title=Lost in Translation (2003) - Filming & production - IMDb | website=[[IMDb]] }}{{cite web | url=https://tokyofox.net/2022/03/15/kyoto-filming-locations-lost-in-translation-2003/ | title=Kyoto Filming Locations: Lost in Translation (2003) | date=15 March 2022 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/kyoto-voice/filming-locations-kyoto/ | title=Filming Locations in Kyoto }}{{cite web | url=https://www.focusfeatures.com/article/film-locations_japan_lost-in-translation | title=Finding Japan in Lost in Translation }} == Gallery == {{Gallery |File:Kyoto Nanzenji06n3200.jpg|Hattō |File:Nanzen-in.JPG|Nanzen-in |File:Nanzenji_temple_-_IMG_5429.JPG|Small Hōjō garden |File:Zen_Garden,_Nanzen-ji_Temple_(7005735830)_(3).jpg|Another part of the complex |File:NanzenjiMomiji.jpg|In the fall }} == See also == *[[Ishikawa Goemon]] * [[List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] 978-0-7112-3447-5}} == External links == * {{Commons category-inline|Nanzen-ji}} * [https://web.archive.org/web/20081217034816/http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/ Kyoto Prefectural Tourism Guide]: {{for|the Buddhist temple in Shanxi Province, China|Nanchan Temple}} {{Short description|Zen Buddhist Temple in Kyoto, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Nanzen-ji
南禅寺 | native_name = | image = File:Kyoto_Nanzenji01s5s4272.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = The [[sanmon]] | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 86 Nanzenji Fukuchichō, [[Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|Sakyō-ku]], [[Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|0|43.14|N|135|47|39.8|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = Nanzen-ji [[Rinzai]] | deity = [[Shaka Nyorai]] (Śākyamuni) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head Temple | website = {{url|http://www.nanzen.net/english/index.html}} | founded_by = [[Emperor Kameyama]] and [[Mukan Fumon]] | year_completed = 1909 (Reconstruction) }} {{nihongo|'''Nanzen-ji'''|南禅寺|''Nanzen-ji''}}, or '''Zuiryusan Nanzen-ji''', formerly {{nihongo|'''Zenrin-ji'''|禅林寺|''Zenrin-ji''}}, is a [[Zen]] [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] [[temple]] in [[Kyoto, Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. [[Emperor Kameyama]] established it in 1291 on the site of his previous detached palace. It is also the headquarters of the Nanzen-ji branch of [[Rinzai Zen]]. The precincts of Nanzen-ji are a [[Cultural Properties of Japan|nationally designated]] [[Monuments of Japan|Historic Site]] and the Hōjō gardens a [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/00003441 |title=南禅寺境内 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1705 |title=南禅寺方丈庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012}} The temple was destroyed in a fire in 1895 and rebuilt in 1909. ==History== Nanzen-ji was founded in the middle [[Kamakura period]] (1291, or [[Shōō (Kamakura period)|Shōō]] 4 in the [[Japanese era name|Japanese era]] system).{{Cite book|title = Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794-1869|last = Ponsonby-Fane|first = Richard|year = 1956|pages = 154|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=9aLpAAAAMAAJ}} It was destroyed by fire in 1393, 1447, and 1467, rebuilt in 1597, and expanded in the Edo era.{{Cite book|title = Kyoto: a Cultural Guide|last = Martin|first = John and Phyllis|publisher = Tuttle Publishing|year = 2013|isbn = 9781462908172|pages = 177}} A large complex, it has varied over time between nine and twelve sub-temples. [[Zenkei Shibayama]], who provided a popular commentary on the [[Mumonkan]], was an abbot of the monastery. == Significance in Zen Buddhism == Nanzen-ji is not itself considered one of the "five great Zen temples of Kyoto"; however, it does play an important role in the "[[Five Mountain System]]" which was modified from Chinese roots. {{Nihongo|[[Tenryū-ji]]|天龍寺|''Tenryū-ji''}} is considered to be one of the so-called {{nihongo|[[Five Mountain System|Kyoto ''Gozan'']]|京都五山|''Kyōto gozan''}} or "five great Zen temples of Kyoto", along with {{nihongo|[[Shōkoku-ji]]|相国寺|''Shōkoku-ji''}}, {{nihongo|[[Kennin-ji]]|建仁寺|''Kennin-ji''}}, {{nihongo|[[Tōfuku-ji]]|東福寺|''Tōfuku-ji''}}, and {{nihongo|[[Manju-ji]]|満寿寺|''Manju-ji''}}. The head temple presiding over the ''Gozan'' in Kyoto is Nanzen-ji.{{Cite book|title = The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Zen Buddhism|last = Baroni|first = Helen Josephine|publisher = The Rosen Publishing Group|year = 2002|isbn = 9780823922406|pages = 116|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=smNM4ElP3XgC}} After the completion of [[Shōkoku-ji]] by [[Ashikaga Yoshimitsu]] in 1386, a new ranking system was created with Nanzen-ji at the top and in a class of its own. Nanzen-ji had the title of "First Temple of The Land" and played a supervising role.{{Cite book|title = Zen Buddhism: A History (Japan)|last = Dumoulin|first = Heinrich|publisher = World Wisdom, Inc.|year = 2005|isbn = 9780941532907|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=hfMkpD_Xr3sC}} {| class="wikitable" | colspan="3" align="center" | Nanzen-ji |- |  !Kyoto !Kamakura |- |First Rank |[[Tenryū-ji]] |[[Kenchō-ji]] |- |Second Rank |[[Shōkoku-ji]] |[[Engaku-ji]] |- |Third Rank |[[Kennin-ji]] |[[Jufuku-ji]] |- |Fourth Rank |[[Tōfuku-ji]] |[[Jōchi-ji]] |- |Fifth Rank |[[Manju-ji]] |[[Jōmyō-ji (Kamakura)|Jōmyō-ji]] |- |} == Notable structures == === Sanmon === [[File:Nanzenji Sanmon 1.jpg|thumb|View from the second story of the Nanzen-ji Sanmon]] The temple's Sanmon gate was originally constructed in the 13th century, destroyed in 1369 at the order of the government, and reconstructed in 1628.{{Cite book|title = Daitokuji: The Visual Cultures of a Zen Monastery|last = Levine|first = Gregory P.A.|publisher = University of Washington Press|year = 2005|isbn = 9780295985404|pages = [https://archive.org/details/daitokujivisualc0000levi/page/94 94]|url = https://archive.org/details/daitokujivisualc0000levi|url-access = registration}} The gate contains stairs to an elevated viewing area, which was the setting for a famous scene in the 1778 [[Kabuki]] play [[Sanmon Gosan no Kiri]]{{Cite book|title = Historical Dictionary of Japanese Traditional Theatre|last = Leiter|first = Samuel L.|publisher = Rowman & Littlefield|year = 2014|isbn = 9781442239111|pages = 476|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=hCkvBQAAQBAJ}} inspired by the story of the criminal [[Ishikawa Goemon]] who is said to have spoken of the beauty of the view (but who was executed prior to the construction of the current gate). === Hōjō === The hōjō (abbot's quarters) of Nanzen-ji is notable both for its gardens and its art. The garden of the hōjō is considered one of the most significant examples of [[Japanese rock garden|karesansui gardens]] (rock gardens), and was built in the 1600s by [[Kobori Masakazu|Kobori Enshu]]. The garden mirrors natural forms, and is seventy percent gravel.{{Cite book|title = Art Of Japanese Gardens|last = Kuck|first = Loraine|publisher = Routledge|year = 2006|isbn = 9781136213625|pages = 230|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=DZ9FAAAAQBAJ}} It has been designated a national Place of Scenic Beauty. The hōjō itself, also known as the Seiryo-den, was given to the temple by the [[Emperor Go-Yōzei]].{{Cite book|title = The Temples of Kyoto|last = Richie|first = Donald|publisher = Tuttle Publishing|year = 2013|isbn = 9781462908578|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=ChTRAgAAQBAJ}} It contains a variety of important screen paintings on gold backgrounds, including two of tigers by [[Kanō Tan'yū]].{{Cite book|title = Of Gardens: Selected Essays|last = Deitz|first = Paula|publisher = University of Pennsylvania Press|year = 2011|isbn = 9780812206968|pages = 301|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=H9xzdCNIXvYC}} It has been designated a National Treasure. {{Gallery |File:Nanzen-ji_Ⅱ.jpg|The Hōjō |File:Nanzen-ji Ⅲ.jpg|The Hōjō garden |File:Hojo,_Konchi-in_-_IMG_5212.JPG|Crane paintings from inside the Hōjō}} === Nanzen-ji Aqueduct === Constructed in 1890 through the temple grounds to carry irrigation water from the [[Lake Biwa Canal]]. {{Gallery |File:Nanzenji aqueduct - IIMG 5411.JPG|Nanzen-ji aqueduct}} ==In media== Parts of the 2003 film ''[[Lost in Translation (film)|Lost in Translation]]'' were filmed there.{{cite web | url=https://www.imdb.com/title/tt0335266/locations/ | title=Lost in Translation (2003) - Filming & production - IMDb | website=[[IMDb]] }}{{cite web | url=https://tokyofox.net/2022/03/15/kyoto-filming-locations-lost-in-translation-2003/ | title=Kyoto Filming Locations: Lost in Translation (2003) | date=15 March 2022 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/kyoto-voice/filming-locations-kyoto/ | title=Filming Locations in Kyoto }}{{cite web | url=https://www.focusfeatures.com/article/film-locations_japan_lost-in-translation | title=Finding Japan in Lost in Translation }} == Gallery == {{Gallery |File:Kyoto Nanzenji06n3200.jpg|Hattō |File:Nanzen-in.JPG|Nanzen-in |File:Nanzenji_temple_-_IMG_5429.JPG|Small Hōjō garden |File:Zen_Garden,_Nanzen-ji_Temple_(7005735830)_(3).jpg|Another part of the complex |File:NanzenjiMomiji.jpg|In the fall }} == See also == *[[Ishikawa Goemon]] * [[List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] ==References== {{Reflist|2}} ==Further reading== *{{cite book |last= Kawaguchi |first= Yoko |title= Japanese Zen Gardens |location= London |publisher= Francis Lincoln |date= 2014 |isbn= 978-0-7112-3447-5}} == External links == * {{Commons category-inline|Nanzen-ji}} * [https://web.archive.org/web/20081217034816/http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/ Kyoto Prefectural Tourism Guide]: [https://web.archive.org/web/20081221164657/http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/temples/m_nanzenji/ Nanzen-ji] * [http://zen.rinnou.net/index.html Joint Council for Japanese Rinzai and Obaku Zen]: [http://zen.rinnou.net/head_temples/03nanzen.html Nanzen-ji] * [http://www.sacred-destinations.com/japan/kyoto.htm Sacred Destinations]: [http://www.sacred-destinations.com/japan/kyoto-nanzenji.htm Nanzen-ji, Kyoto]; * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110220214752/http://www.buddhistravel.com/index.php?kyoto Buddhist Travel]: [https://web.archive.org/web/20160303203607/http://www.buddhistravel.com/index.php?id=120,284,0,0,1,0 Nanzen-ji] *[https://www.accessible-japan.com/places/japan/kyoto/kyoto/attractions/nanzenji-temple/ Wheelchair Accessibility of Nanzenji] {{Buddhism topics}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{Authority control}} of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:1290s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Emperor Kameyama]] established it in 1291 on the site of his previous detached palace. It is also the headquarters of the Nanzen-ji branch of [[Rinzai Zen]]. The precincts of Nanzen-ji are a [[Cultural Properties of Japan|nationally designated]] [[Monuments of Japan|Historic Site]] and the Hōjō gardens a [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/00003441 |title=南禅寺境内 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1705 |title=南禅寺方丈庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012}} The temple was destroyed in a fire in 1895 and rebuilt in 1909. ==History== Nanzen-ji was founded in the middle [[Kamakura period]] (1291, or [[Shōō (Kamakura period)|Shōō]] 4 in the [[Japanese era name|Japanese era]] system).{{Cite book|title = Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794-1869|last = Ponsonby-Fane|first = Richard|year = 1956|pages = 154|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=9aLpAAAAMAAJ}} It was destroyed by fire in 1393, 1447, and 1467, rebuilt in 1597, and expanded in the Edo era.{{Cite book|title = Kyoto: a Cultural Guide|last = Martin|first = John and Phyllis|publisher = Tuttle Publishing|year = 2013|isbn = 9781462908172|pages = 177}} A large complex, it has varied over time between nine and twelve sub-temples. [[Zenkei Shibayama]], who provided a popular commentary on the [[Mumonkan]], was an abbot of the monastery. == Significance in Zen Buddhism == Nanzen-ji is not itself considered one of the "five great Zen temples of Kyoto"; however, it does play an important role in the "[[Five Mountain System]]" which was modified from Chinese roots. {{Nihongo|[[Tenryū-ji]]|天龍寺|''Tenryū-ji''}} is considered to be one of the so-called {{nihongo|[[Five Mountain System|Kyoto ''Gozan'']]|京都五山|''Kyōto gozan''}} or "five great Zen temples of Kyoto", along with {{nihongo|[[Shōkoku-ji]]|相国寺|''Shōkoku-ji''}}, {{nihongo|[[Kennin-ji]]|建仁寺|''Kennin-ji''}}, {{nihongo|[[Tōfuku-ji]]|東福寺|''Tōfuku-ji''}}, and {{nihongo|[[Manju-ji]]|満寿寺|''Manju-ji''}}. The head temple presiding over the ''Gozan'' in Kyoto is Nanzen-ji.{{Cite book|title = The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Zen Buddhism|last = Baroni|first = Helen Josephine|publisher = The Rosen Publishing Group|year = 2002|isbn = 9780823922406|pages = 116|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=smNM4ElP3XgC}} After the completion of [[Shōkoku-ji]] by [[Ashikaga Yoshimitsu]] in 1386, a new ranking system was created with Nanzen-ji at the top and in a class of its own. Nanzen-ji had the title of "First Temple of The Land" and played a supervising role.{{Cite book|title = Zen Buddhism: A History (Japan)|last = Dumoulin|first = Heinrich|publisher = World Wisdom, Inc.|year = 2005|isbn = 9780941532907|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=hfMkpD_Xr3sC}} {| class="wikitable" | colspan="3" align="center" | Nanzen-ji |- |  !Kyoto !Kamakura |- |First Rank |[[Tenryū-ji]] |[[Kenchō-ji]] |- |Second Rank |[[Shōkoku-ji]] |[[Engaku-ji]] |- |Third Rank |[[Kennin-ji]] |[[Jufuku-ji]] |- |Fourth Rank |[[Tōfuku-ji]] |[[Jōchi-ji]] |- |Fifth Rank |[[Manju-ji]] |[[Jōmyō-ji (Kamakura)|Jōmyō-ji]] |- |} == Notable structures == === Sanmon === [[File:Nanzenji Sanmon 1.jpg|thumb|View from the second story of the Nanzen-ji Sanmon]] The temple's Sanmon gate was originally constructed in the 13th century, destroyed in 1369 at the order of the government, and reconstructed in 1628.{{Cite book|title = Daitokuji: The Visual Cultures of a Zen Monastery|last = Levine|first = Gregory P.A.|publisher = University of Washington Press|year = 2005|isbn = 9780295985404|pages = [https://archive.org/details/daitokujivisualc0000levi/page/94 94]|url = https://archive.org/details/daitokujivisualc0000levi|url-access = registration}} The gate contains stairs to an elevated viewing area, which was the setting for a famous scene in the 1778 [[Kabuki]] play [[Sanmon Gosan no Kiri]]{{Cite book|title = Historical Dictionary of Japanese Traditional Theatre|last = Leiter|first = Samuel L.|publisher = Rowman & Littlefield|year = 2014|isbn = 9781442239111|pages = 476|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=hCkvBQAAQBAJ}} inspired by the story of the criminal [[Ishikawa Goemon]] who is said to have spoken of the beauty of the view (but who was executed prior to the construction of the current gate). === Hōjō === The hōjō (abbot's quarters) of Nanzen-ji is notable both for its gardens and its art. The garden of the hōjō is considered one of the most significant examples of [[Japanese rock garden|karesansui gardens]] (rock gardens), and was built in the 1600s by [[Kobori Masakazu|Kobori Enshu]]. The garden mirrors natural forms, and is seventy percent gravel.{{Cite book|title = Art Of Japanese Gardens|last = Kuck|first = Loraine|publisher = Routledge|year = 2006|isbn = 9781136213625|pages = 230|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=DZ9FAAAAQBAJ}} It has been designated a national Place of Scenic Beauty. Publishing|year = 2013|isbn = 9781462908578|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=ChTRAgAAQBAJ}} It contains a variety of important screen paintings on gold backgrounds, including two of tigers by [[Kanō Tan'yū]].{{Cite book|title = Of Gardens: Selected Essays|last = Deitz|first = Paula|publisher = University of Pennsylvania Press|year = 2011|isbn = 9780812206968|pages = 301|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=H9xzdCNIXvYC}} It has been designated a National Treasure. {{Gallery |File:Nanzen-ji_Ⅱ.jpg|The Hōjō |File:Nanzen-ji Ⅲ.jpg|The Hōjō garden |File:Hojo,_Konchi-in_-_IMG_5212.JPG|Crane paintings from inside the Hōjō}} === Nanzen-ji Aqueduct === Constructed in 1890 through the temple grounds to carry irrigation water from the [[Lake Biwa Canal]]. {{Gallery |File:Nanzenji aqueduct - IIMG 5411.JPG|Nanzen-ji aqueduct}} ==In media== Parts of the 2003 film ''[[Lost in Translation (film)|Lost in Translation]]'' were filmed there.{{cite web url=https://tokyofox.net/2022/03/15/kyoto-filming-locations-lost-in-translation-2003/ | title=Kyoto Filming Locations: Lost in Translation (2003) | date=15 March 2022 }}{{cite web | |File:Nanzen-in.JPG|Nanzen-in |File:Nanzenji_temple_-_IMG_5429.JPG|Small Hōjō garden |File:Zen_Garden,_Nanzen-ji_Temple_(7005735830)_(3).jpg|Another part of the complex |File:NanzenjiMomiji.jpg|In the of Scenic Beauty]] [[Category:Historic Sites of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:1290s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Nanzen-ji]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]]

{{for|the Buddhist temple in Shanxi Province, China|Nanchan Temple}} {{Short description|Zen Buddhist Temple in Kyoto, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Nanzen-ji
南禅寺 | native_name = | image = File:Kyoto_Nanzenji01s5s4272.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = The [[sanmon]] | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 86 Nanzenji Fukuchichō, [[Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|Sakyō-ku]], [[Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|0|43.14|N|135|47|39.8|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = Nanzen-ji [[Rinzai]] | deity = [[Shaka Nyorai]] (Śākyamuni) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head Temple | website = {{url|http://www.nanzen.net/english/index.html}} | founded_by = [[Emperor Kameyama]] and [[Mukan Fumon]] | year_completed = 1909 (Reconstruction) }} {{nihongo|'''Nanzen-ji'''|南禅寺|''Nanzen-ji''}}, or '''Zuiryusan Nanzen-ji''', formerly {{nihongo|'''Zenrin-ji'''|禅林寺|''Zenrin-ji''}}, is a [[Zen]] [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] [[temple]] in [[Kyoto, Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. [[Emperor Kameyama]] established it in 1291 on the site of his previous detached palace. It is also the headquarters of the Nanzen-ji branch of [[Rinzai Zen]]. The precincts of Nanzen-ji are a [[Cultural Properties of Japan|nationally designated]] [[Monuments of Japan|Historic Site]] and the Hōjō gardens a [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/00003441 |title=南禅寺境内 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1705 |title=南禅寺方丈庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012}} The temple was destroyed in a fire in 1895 and rebuilt in 1909. ==History== Nanzen-ji was founded in the middle [[Kamakura period]] (1291, or [[Shōō (Kamakura period)|Shōō]] 4 in the [[Japanese era name|Japanese era]] system).{{Cite book|title = Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794-1869|last = Ponsonby-Fane|first = Richard|year = 1956|pages = 154|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=9aLpAAAAMAAJ}} It was destroyed by fire in 1393, 1447, and 1467, rebuilt in 1597, and expanded in the Edo era.{{Cite book|title = Kyoto: a Cultural Guide|last = Martin|first = John and Phyllis|publisher = Tuttle Publishing|year = 2013|isbn = 9781462908172|pages = 177}} A large complex, it has varied over time between nine and twelve sub-temples. [[Zenkei Shibayama]], who provided a popular commentary on the [[Mumonkan]], was an abbot of the monastery. == Significance in Zen Buddhism == Nanzen-ji is not itself considered one of the "five great Zen temples of Kyoto"; however, it does play an important role in the "[[Five Mountain System]]" which was modified from Chinese roots. {{Nihongo|[[Tenryū-ji]]|天龍寺|''Tenryū-ji''}} is considered to be one of the so-called {{nihongo|[[Five Mountain System|Kyoto ''Gozan'']]|京都五山|''Kyōto gozan''}} or "five great Zen temples of Kyoto", along with {{nihongo|[[Shōkoku-ji]]|相国寺|''Shōkoku-ji''}}, {{nihongo|[[Kennin-ji]]|建仁寺|''Kennin-ji''}}, {{nihongo|[[Tōfuku-ji]]|東福寺|''Tōfuku-ji''}}, and {{nihongo|[[Manju-ji]]|満寿寺|''Manju-ji''}}. The head temple presiding over the ''Gozan'' in Kyoto is Nanzen-ji.{{Cite book|title = The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Zen Buddhism|last = Baroni|first = Helen Josephine|publisher = The Rosen Publishing Group|year = 2002|isbn = 9780823922406|pages = 116|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=smNM4ElP3XgC}} After the completion of [[Shōkoku-ji]] by [[Ashikaga Yoshimitsu]] in 1386, a new ranking system was created with Nanzen-ji at the top and in a class of its own. Nanzen-ji had the title of "First Temple of The Land" and played a supervising role.{{Cite book|title = Zen Buddhism: A History (Japan)|last = Dumoulin|first = Heinrich|publisher = World Wisdom, Inc.|year = 2005|isbn = 9780941532907|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=hfMkpD_Xr3sC}} {| class="wikitable" | colspan="3" align="center" | Nanzen-ji |- |  !Kyoto !Kamakura |- |First Rank |[[Tenryū-ji]] |[[Kenchō-ji]] |- |Second Rank |[[Shōkoku-ji]] |[[Engaku-ji]] |- |Third Rank |[[Kennin-ji]] |[[Jufuku-ji]] |- |Fourth Rank |[[Tōfuku-ji]] |[[Jōchi-ji]] |- |Fifth Rank |[[Manju-ji]] |[[Jōmyō-ji (Kamakura)|Jōmyō-ji]] |- |} == Notable structures == === Sanmon === [[File:Nanzenji Sanmon 1.jpg|thumb|View from the second story of the Nanzen-ji Sanmon]] The temple's Sanmon gate was originally constructed in the 13th century, destroyed in 1369 at the order of the government, and reconstructed in 1628.{{Cite book|title = Daitokuji: The Visual Cultures of a Zen Monastery|last = Levine|first = Gregory P.A.|publisher = University of Washington Press|year = 2005|isbn = 9780295985404|pages = [https://archive.org/details/daitokujivisualc0000levi/page/94 94]|url = https://archive.org/details/daitokujivisualc0000levi|url-access = registration}} The gate contains stairs to an elevated viewing area, which was the setting for a famous scene in the 1778 [[Kabuki]] play [[Sanmon Gosan no Kiri]]{{Cite book|title = Historical Dictionary of Japanese Traditional Theatre|last = Leiter|first = Samuel L.|publisher = Rowman & Littlefield|year = 2014|isbn = 9781442239111|pages = 476|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=hCkvBQAAQBAJ}} inspired by the story of the criminal [[Ishikawa Goemon]] who is said to have spoken of the beauty of the view (but who was executed prior to the construction of the current gate). === Hōjō === The hōjō (abbot's quarters) of Nanzen-ji is notable both for its gardens and its art. The garden of the hōjō is considered one of the most significant examples of [[Japanese rock garden|karesansui gardens]] (rock gardens), and was built in the 1600s by [[Kobori Masakazu|Kobori Enshu]]. The garden mirrors natural forms, and is seventy percent gravel.{{Cite book|title = Art Of Japanese Gardens|last = Kuck|first = Loraine|publisher = Routledge|year = 2006|isbn = 9781136213625|pages = 230|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=DZ9FAAAAQBAJ}} It has been designated a national Place of Scenic Beauty. The hōjō itself, also known as the Seiryo-den, was given to the temple by the [[Emperor Go-Yōzei]].{{Cite book|title = The Temples of Kyoto|last = Richie|first = Donald|publisher = Tuttle Publishing|year = 2013|isbn = 9781462908578|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=ChTRAgAAQBAJ}} It contains a variety of important screen paintings on gold backgrounds, including two of tigers by [[Kanō Tan'yū]].{{Cite book|title = Of Gardens: Selected Essays|last = Deitz|first = Paula|publisher = University of Pennsylvania Press|year = 2011|isbn = 9780812206968|pages = 301|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=H9xzdCNIXvYC}} It has been designated a National Treasure. {{Gallery |File:Nanzen-ji_Ⅱ.jpg|The Hōjō |File:Nanzen-ji Ⅲ.jpg|The Hōjō garden |File:Hojo,_Konchi-in_-_IMG_5212.JPG|Crane paintings from inside the Hōjō}} === Nanzen-ji Aqueduct === Constructed in 1890 through the temple grounds to carry irrigation water from the [[Lake Biwa Canal]]. {{Gallery |File:Nanzenji aqueduct - IIMG 5411.JPG|Nanzen-ji aqueduct}} ==In media== Parts of the 2003 film ''[[Lost in Translation (film)|Lost in Translation]]'' were filmed there.{{cite web | url=https://www.imdb.com/title/tt0335266/locations/ | title=Lost in Translation (2003) - Filming & production - IMDb | website=[[IMDb]] }}{{cite web | url=https://tokyofox.net/2022/03/15/kyoto-filming-locations-lost-in-translation-2003/ | title=Kyoto Filming Locations: Lost in Translation (2003) | date=15 March 2022 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/kyoto-voice/filming-locations-kyoto/ | title=Filming Locations in Kyoto }}{{cite web | url=https://www.focusfeatures.com/article/film-locations_japan_lost-in-translation | title=Finding Japan in Lost in Translation }} == Gallery == {{Gallery |File:Kyoto Nanzenji06n3200.jpg|Hattō |File:Nanzen-in.JPG|Nanzen-in |File:Nanzenji_temple_-_IMG_5429.JPG|Small Hōjō garden |File:Zen_Garden,_Nanzen-ji_Temple_(7005735830)_(3).jpg|Another part of the complex |File:NanzenjiMomiji.jpg|In the fall }} == See also == *[[Ishikawa Goemon]] * [[List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] The head temple presiding over the ''Gozan'' in Kyoto is Nanzen-ji.{{Cite book|title = The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Zen Buddhism|last = Baroni|first = Helen Josephine|publisher = The Rosen Publishing Group|year = 2002|isbn = 9780823922406|pages = 116|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=smNM4ElP3XgC}} After the completion of [[Shōkoku-ji]] by [[Ashikaga Yoshimitsu]] in 1386, a new ranking system was created with Nanzen-ji at the top and in a class of its own. The garden mirrors natural forms, and is seventy percent gravel.{{Cite book|title = Art Of Japanese Gardens|last = Kuck|first = Loraine|publisher = Routledge|year = 2006|isbn = 9781136213625|pages = 230|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=DZ9FAAAAQBAJ}} It has been designated a national Place of Scenic Beauty. 南禅寺 | native_name = | image = File:Kyoto_Nanzenji01s5s4272.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = The [[sanmon]] | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 86 Nanzenji Fukuchichō, [[Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|Sakyō-ku]], [[Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|0|43.14|N|135|47|39.8|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = Nanzen-ji [[Rinzai]] | deity = [[Shaka Nyorai]] (Śākyamuni) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head Temple | website = {{url|http://www.nanzen.net/english/index.html}} | founded_by = [[Emperor Kameyama]] and [[Mukan Fumon]] | year_completed = 1909 (Reconstruction) }} {{nihongo|'''Nanzen-ji'''|南禅寺|''Nanzen-ji''}}, or '''Zuiryusan Nanzen-ji''', formerly {{nihongo|'''Zenrin-ji'''|禅林寺|''Zenrin-ji''}}, is a [[Zen]] [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] [[temple]] in [[Kyoto, Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. [[Emperor Kameyama]] established it in 1291 on the site of his previous detached palace. It is also the headquarters of the Nanzen-ji branch of [[Rinzai Zen]]. The precincts of Nanzen-ji are a [[Cultural Properties of Japan|nationally designated]] [[Monuments of Japan|Historic Site]] and the Hōjō gardens a [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/00003441 |title=南禅寺境内 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1705 |title=南禅寺方丈庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012}} The temple was destroyed in a fire in 1895 and rebuilt in 1909. ==History== Nanzen-ji was founded in the middle [[Kamakura period]] (1291, or [[Shōō (Kamakura period)|Shōō]] 4 in the [[Japanese era name|Japanese era]] system).{{Cite book|title = Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794-1869|last = Ponsonby-Fane|first = Richard|year = 1956|pages = 154|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=9aLpAAAAMAAJ}} It was destroyed by fire in 1393, 1447, and 1467, rebuilt in 1597, and expanded in the Edo era.{{Cite book|title = Kyoto: a Cultural Guide|last = Martin|first = John and Phyllis|publisher = Tuttle Publishing|year = 2013|isbn = 9781462908172|pages = 177}} A large complex, it has varied over time between nine and twelve sub-temples. [[Zenkei Shibayama]], who provided a popular commentary on the [[Mumonkan]], was an abbot of the monastery. == Significance in Zen Buddhism == Nanzen-ji is not itself considered one of the "five great Zen temples of Kyoto"; however, it does play an important role in the "[[Five Mountain System]]" which was modified from Chinese roots. {{Nihongo|[[Tenryū-ji]]|天龍寺|''Tenryū-ji''}} is considered to be one of the so-called {{nihongo|[[Five Mountain System|Kyoto ''Gozan'']]|京都五山|''Kyōto gozan''}} or "five great Zen temples of Kyoto", along with {{nihongo|[[Shōkoku-ji]]|相国寺|''Shōkoku-ji''}}, {{nihongo|[[Kennin-ji]]|建仁寺|''Kennin-ji''}}, {{nihongo|[[Tōfuku-ji]]|東福寺|''Tōfuku-ji''}}, and {{nihongo|[[Manju-ji]]|満寿寺|''Manju-ji''}}. The head temple presiding over the ''Gozan'' in Kyoto is Nanzen-ji.{{Cite book|title = The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Zen Buddhism|last = Baroni|first = Helen Josephine|publisher = The Rosen Publishing Group|year = 2002|isbn = 9780823922406|pages = 116|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=smNM4ElP3XgC}} After the completion of [[Shōkoku-ji]] by [[Ashikaga Yoshimitsu]] in 1386, a new ranking system was created with Nanzen-ji at the top and in a class of its own. Nanzen-ji had the title of "First Temple of The Land" and played a supervising role.{{Cite book|title = Zen Buddhism: A History (Japan)|last = Dumoulin|first = Heinrich|publisher = World Wisdom, Inc.|year = 2005|isbn = 9780941532907|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=hfMkpD_Xr3sC}} {| class="wikitable" | colspan="3" align="center" | Nanzen-ji |- |  !Kyoto !Kamakura |- |First Rank |[[Tenryū-ji]] |[[Kenchō-ji]] |- |Second Rank |[[Shōkoku-ji]] |[[Engaku-ji]] |- |Third Rank |[[Kennin-ji]] |[[Jufuku-ji]] |- |Fourth Rank |[[Tōfuku-ji]] |[[Jōchi-ji]] |- |Fifth Rank |[[Manju-ji]] |[[Jōmyō-ji (Kamakura)|Jōmyō-ji]] |- |} == Notable structures == === Sanmon === [[File:Nanzenji Sanmon 1.jpg|thumb|View from the second story of the Nanzen-ji Sanmon]] The temple's Sanmon gate was originally constructed in the 13th century, destroyed in 1369 at the order of the government, and reconstructed in 1628.{{Cite book|title = Daitokuji: The Visual Cultures of a Zen Monastery|last = Levine|first = Gregory P.A.|publisher = University of Washington Press|year = 2005|isbn = 9780295985404|pages = [https://archive.org/details/daitokujivisualc0000levi/page/94 94]|url = https://archive.org/details/daitokujivisualc0000levi|url-access = registration}} The gate contains stairs to an elevated viewing area, which was the setting for a famous scene in the 1778 [[Kabuki]] play [[Sanmon Gosan no Kiri]]{{Cite book|title = Historical Dictionary of Japanese Traditional Theatre|last = Leiter|first = Samuel L.|publisher = Rowman & Littlefield|year = 2014|isbn = 9781442239111|pages = 476|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=hCkvBQAAQBAJ}} inspired by the story of the criminal [[Ishikawa Goemon]] who is said to have spoken of the beauty of the view (but who was executed prior to the construction of the current gate). === Hōjō === The hōjō (abbot's quarters) of Nanzen-ji is notable both for its gardens and its art. The garden of the hōjō is considered one of the most significant examples of [[Japanese rock garden|karesansui gardens]] (rock gardens), and was built in the 1600s by [[Kobori Masakazu|Kobori Enshu]]. The garden mirrors natural forms, and is seventy percent gravel.{{Cite book|title = Art Of Japanese Gardens|last = Kuck|first = Loraine|publisher = Routledge|year = 2006|isbn = 9781136213625|pages = 230|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=DZ9FAAAAQBAJ}} It has been designated a national Place of Scenic Beauty. The hōjō itself, also known as the Seiryo-den, was given to the temple by the [[Emperor Go-Yōzei]].{{Cite book|title = The Temples of Kyoto|last = Richie|first = Donald|publisher = Tuttle Publishing|year = 2013|isbn = 9781462908578|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=ChTRAgAAQBAJ}} It contains a variety of important screen paintings on gold backgrounds, including two of tigers by [[Kanō Tan'yū]].{{Cite book|title = Of Gardens: Selected Essays|last = Deitz|first = Paula|publisher = University of Pennsylvania Press|year = 2011|isbn = 9780812206968|pages = 301|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=H9xzdCNIXvYC}} It has been designated a National Treasure. {{Gallery |File:Nanzen-ji_Ⅱ.jpg|The Hōjō |File:Nanzen-ji Ⅲ.jpg|The Hōjō garden |File:Hojo,_Konchi-in_-_IMG_5212.JPG|Crane paintings from inside the Hōjō}} === Nanzen-ji Aqueduct === Constructed in 1890 through the temple grounds to carry irrigation water from the [[Lake Biwa Canal]]. {{Gallery |File:Nanzenji aqueduct - IIMG 5411.JPG|Nanzen-ji aqueduct}} ==In media== Parts of the 2003 film ''[[Lost in Translation (film)|Lost in Translation]]'' were filmed there.{{cite web | url=https://www.imdb.com/title/tt0335266/locations/ | title=Lost in Translation (2003) - Filming & production - IMDb | website=[[IMDb]] }}{{cite web | url=https://tokyofox.net/2022/03/15/kyoto-filming-locations-lost-in-translation-2003/ | title=Kyoto Filming Locations: Lost in Translation (2003) | date=15 March 2022 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/kyoto-voice/filming-locations-kyoto/ | title=Filming Locations in Kyoto }}{{cite web | url=https://www.focusfeatures.com/article/film-locations_japan_lost-in-translation | title=Finding Japan in Lost in Translation }} == Gallery == {{Gallery |File:Kyoto Nanzenji06n3200.jpg|Hattō |File:Nanzen-in.JPG|Nanzen-in |File:Nanzenji_temple_-_IMG_5429.JPG|Small Hōjō garden |File:Zen_Garden,_Nanzen-ji_Temple_(7005735830)_(3).jpg|Another part of the complex |File:NanzenjiMomiji.jpg|In the fall }} == See also == *[[Ishikawa Goemon]] * [[List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] ==References== {{Reflist|2}} ==Further reading== *{{cite book |last= Kawaguchi |first= Yoko |title= Japanese Zen Gardens |location= London |publisher= Francis Lincoln |date= 2014 |isbn= 978-0-7112-3447-5}} == External links == * {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia 2]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia Rinzai and Obaku Zen]: [http://zen.rinnou.net/head_temples/03nanzen.html Nanzen-ji] * [http://www.sacred-destinations.com/japan/kyoto.htm Sacred Destinations]: [[Emperor Kameyama]] established it in 1291 on the site of his previous detached palace. It is also the headquarters of the Nanzen-ji branch of [[Rinzai Zen]]. The precincts of Nanzen-ji are a [[Cultural Properties of Japan|nationally designated]] [[Monuments of Japan|Historic Site]] and the Hōjō gardens a [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/00003441 |title=南禅寺境内 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1705 |title=南禅寺方丈庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012}} The temple was destroyed in a fire in 1895 and rebuilt in 1909. ==History== Nanzen-ji was founded in the middle [[Kamakura period]] (1291, or [[Shōō (Kamakura period)|Shōō]] 4 in the [[Japanese era name|Japanese era]] system).{{Cite book|title = Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794-1869|last = Ponsonby-Fane|first = Richard|year = 1956|pages = 154|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=9aLpAAAAMAAJ}} It was destroyed by fire in 1393, 1447, and 1467, rebuilt in 1597, and expanded in the Edo era.{{Cite book|title = Kyoto: a Cultural Guide|last = Martin|first = John and Phyllis|publisher = Tuttle Publishing|year = 2013|isbn = 9781462908172|pages = 177}} A large complex, it has varied over time between nine and twelve sub-temples. [[Zenkei Shibayama]], who provided a popular commentary on the [[Mumonkan]], was an abbot of the monastery. == Significance in Zen Buddhism == Nanzen-ji is not itself considered one of the "five great Zen temples of Kyoto"; however, it does play an important role in the "[[Five Mountain System]]" which was modified from Chinese roots. {{Nihongo|[[Tenryū-ji]]|天龍寺|''Tenryū-ji''}} is considered to be one of the so-called {{nihongo|[[Five Mountain System|Kyoto ''Gozan'']]|京都五山|''Kyōto gozan''}} or "five great Zen temples of Kyoto", along with {{nihongo|[[Shōkoku-ji]]|相国寺|''Shōkoku-ji''}}, {{nihongo|[[Kennin-ji]]|建仁寺|''Kennin-ji''}}, {{nihongo|[[Tōfuku-ji]]|東福寺|''Tōfuku-ji''}}, and {{nihongo|[[Manju-ji]]|満寿寺|''Manju-ji''}}. The head temple presiding over the ''Gozan'' in Kyoto is Nanzen-ji.{{Cite book|title = The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Zen Buddhism|last = Baroni|first = Helen Josephine|publisher = The Rosen Publishing Group|year = 2002|isbn = 9780823922406|pages = 116|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=smNM4ElP3XgC}} After the completion of [[Shōkoku-ji]] by [[Ashikaga Yoshimitsu]] in 1386, a new ranking system was created with Nanzen-ji at the top and in a class of its own. Nanzen-ji had the title of "First Temple of The Land" and played a supervising role.{{Cite book|title = Zen Buddhism: A History (Japan)|last = Dumoulin|first = Heinrich|publisher = World Wisdom, Inc.|year = 2005|isbn = 9780941532907|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=hfMkpD_Xr3sC}} {| class="wikitable" | colspan="3" align="center" | Nanzen-ji |- |  !Kyoto !Kamakura |- |First Rank |[[Tenryū-ji]] |[[Kenchō-ji]] |- |Second Rank |[[Shōkoku-ji]] |[[Engaku-ji]] |- |Third Rank |[[Kennin-ji]] |[[Jufuku-ji]] |- |Fourth Rank |[[Tōfuku-ji]] |[[Jōchi-ji]] |- |Fifth Rank |[[Manju-ji]] |[[Jōmyō-ji (Kamakura)|Jōmyō-ji]] |- |} == Notable structures == === Sanmon === [[File:Nanzenji Sanmon 1.jpg|thumb|View from the second story of the Nanzen-ji Sanmon]] The temple's Sanmon gate was originally constructed in the 13th century, destroyed in 1369 at the order of the government, and reconstructed in 1628.{{Cite book|title = Daitokuji: The Visual Cultures of a Zen Monastery|last = Levine|first = Gregory P.A.|publisher = University of Washington Press|year = 2005|isbn = 9780295985404|pages = [https://archive.org/details/daitokujivisualc0000levi/page/94 94]|url = https://archive.org/details/daitokujivisualc0000levi|url-access = registration}} The gate contains stairs to an elevated viewing area, which was the setting for a famous scene in the 1778 [[Kabuki]] play [[Sanmon Gosan no Kiri]]{{Cite book|title = Historical Dictionary of Japanese Traditional Theatre|last = Leiter|first = Samuel L.|publisher = Rowman & Littlefield|year = 2014|isbn = 9781442239111|pages = 476|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=hCkvBQAAQBAJ}} inspired by the story of the criminal [[Ishikawa Goemon]] who is said to have spoken of the beauty of the view (but who was executed prior to the construction of the current gate). === Hōjō === The hōjō (abbot's quarters) of Nanzen-ji is notable both for its gardens and its art. The garden of the hōjō is considered one of the most significant examples of [[Japanese rock garden|karesansui gardens]] (rock gardens), and was built in the 1600s by [[Kobori Masakazu|Kobori Enshu]]. The garden mirrors natural forms, and is seventy percent gravel.{{Cite book|title = Art Of Japanese Gardens|last = Kuck|first = Loraine|publisher = Routledge|year = 2006|isbn = 9781136213625|pages = 230|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=DZ9FAAAAQBAJ}} It has been designated a national Place of Scenic Beauty. The hōjō itself, also known as the Seiryo-den, was given to the temple by the [[Emperor Go-Yōzei]].{{Cite book|title = The Temples of Kyoto|last = Richie|first = Donald|publisher = Tuttle Publishing|year = 2013|isbn = 9781462908578|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=ChTRAgAAQBAJ}} It contains a variety of important screen paintings on gold backgrounds, including two of tigers by [[Kanō Tan'yū]].{{Cite book|title = Of Gardens: Selected Essays|last = Deitz|first = Paula|publisher = University of Pennsylvania Press|year = 2011|isbn = 9780812206968|pages = 301|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=H9xzdCNIXvYC}} It has been designated a National Treasure. {{Gallery |File:Nanzen-ji_Ⅱ.jpg|The Hōjō |File:Nanzen-ji Ⅲ.jpg|The Hōjō garden |File:Hojo,_Konchi-in_-_IMG_5212.JPG|Crane paintings from inside the Hōjō}} === Nanzen-ji Aqueduct === Constructed in 1890 through the temple grounds to carry irrigation water from the [[Lake Biwa Canal]]. {{Gallery |File:Nanzenji aqueduct - IIMG 5411.JPG|Nanzen-ji aqueduct}} ==In media== Parts of the 2003 film ''[[Lost in Translation (film)|Lost in Translation]]'' were filmed there.{{cite web | url=https://www.imdb.com/title/tt0335266/locations/ | title=Lost in Translation (2003) - Filming & production - IMDb | website=[[IMDb]] }}{{cite web | url=https://tokyofox.net/2022/03/15/kyoto-filming-locations-lost-in-translation-2003/ | title=Kyoto Filming Locations: Lost in Translation (2003) | date=15 March 2022 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/kyoto-voice/filming-locations-kyoto/ | title=Filming Locations in Kyoto }}{{cite web | url=https://www.focusfeatures.com/article/film-locations_japan_lost-in-translation | title=Finding Japan in Lost in Translation }} == Gallery == {{Gallery |File:Kyoto Nanzenji06n3200.jpg|Hattō |File:Nanzen-in.JPG|Nanzen-in |File:Nanzenji_temple_-_IMG_5429.JPG|Small Hōjō garden |File:Zen_Garden,_Nanzen-ji_Temple_(7005735830)_(3).jpg|Another part of the complex |File:NanzenjiMomiji.jpg|In the fall }} == See also == *[[Ishikawa Goemon]] * [[List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] The head temple presiding over the ''Gozan'' in Kyoto is Nanzen-ji.{{Cite book|title = The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Zen Buddhism|last = Baroni|first = Helen Josephine|publisher = The Rosen Publishing Group|year = 2002|isbn = 9780823922406|pages = 116|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=smNM4ElP3XgC}} After the completion of [[Shōkoku-ji]] by [[Ashikaga Yoshimitsu]] in 1386, a new ranking system was created with Nanzen-ji at the top and in a class of its own. Nanzen-ji had the title of "First Temple of The Land" and played a supervising role.{{Cite book|title = Zen Buddhism: A History (Japan)|last = Dumoulin|first = Heinrich|publisher = World Wisdom, Inc.|year = 2005|isbn = 9780941532907|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=hfMkpD_Xr3sC}} {| class="wikitable" | colspan="3" align="center" | Nanzen-ji |- |  !Kyoto !Kamakura |- |First Rank |[[Tenryū-ji]] |[[Kenchō-ji]] |- |Second Rank |[[Shōkoku-ji]] |[[Engaku-ji]] |- |Third Rank |[[Kennin-ji]] |[[Jufuku-ji]] |- |Fourth Rank |[[Tōfuku-ji]] |[[Jōchi-ji]] |- |Fifth Rank |[[Manju-ji]] |[[Jōmyō-ji (Kamakura)|Jōmyō-ji]] |- |} == Notable structures == === Sanmon === [[File:Nanzenji Sanmon 1.jpg|thumb|View from the second story of the Nanzen-ji Sanmon]] The temple's Sanmon gate was originally constructed in the 13th century, destroyed in 1369 at the order of the government, and reconstructed in 1628.{{Cite book|title = Daitokuji: The Visual Cultures of a Zen Monastery|last = Levine|first = Gregory P.A.|publisher = University of Washington Press|year = 2005|isbn = 9780295985404|pages = [https://archive.org/details/daitokujivisualc0000levi/page/94 94]|url = https://archive.org/details/daitokujivisualc0000levi|url-access = registration}} The gate contains stairs to an elevated viewing area, which was the setting for a famous scene in the 1778 [[Kabuki]] play [[Sanmon Gosan no Kiri]]{{Cite book|title = Historical Dictionary of Japanese Traditional Theatre|last = Leiter|first = Samuel L.|publisher = Rowman & Littlefield|year = 2014|isbn = 9781442239111|pages = 476|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=hCkvBQAAQBAJ}} inspired by the story of the criminal [[Ishikawa Goemon]] who is said to have spoken of the beauty of the view (but who was executed prior to the construction of the current gate). === Hōjō === The hōjō (abbot's quarters) of Nanzen-ji is notable both for its gardens and its art. The garden of the hōjō is considered one of the most significant examples of [[Japanese rock garden|karesansui gardens]] (rock gardens), and was built in the 1600s by [[Kobori Masakazu|Kobori Enshu]]. The garden mirrors natural forms, and is seventy percent gravel.{{Cite book|title = Art Of Japanese Gardens|last = Kuck|first = Loraine|publisher = Routledge|year = 2006|isbn = 9781136213625|pages = 230|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=DZ9FAAAAQBAJ}} It has been designated a national Place of Scenic Beauty. The hōjō itself, also known as the Seiryo-den, was given to the temple by the [[Emperor Go-Yōzei]].{{Cite book|title = The Temples of Kyoto|last = Richie|first = Donald|publisher = Tuttle Publishing|year = 2013|isbn = 9781462908578|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=ChTRAgAAQBAJ}} It contains a variety of important screen paintings on gold backgrounds, including two of tigers by [[Kanō Tan'yū]].{{Cite book|title = Of Gardens: Selected Essays|last = Deitz|first = Paula|publisher = University of Pennsylvania Press|year = 2011|isbn = 9780812206968|pages = 301|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=H9xzdCNIXvYC}} It has been designated a National Treasure. {{Gallery |File:Nanzen-ji_Ⅱ.jpg|The Hōjō |File:Nanzen-ji Ⅲ.jpg|The Hōjō garden |File:Hojo,_Konchi-in_-_IMG_5212.JPG|Crane paintings from inside the Hōjō}} === Nanzen-ji Aqueduct === Constructed in 1890 through the temple grounds to carry irrigation water from the [[Lake Biwa Canal]]. {{Gallery |File:Nanzenji aqueduct - IIMG 5411.JPG|Nanzen-ji aqueduct}} ==In media== Parts of the 2003 film ''[[Lost in Translation (film)|Lost in Translation]]'' were filmed there.{{cite web | url=https://www.imdb.com/title/tt0335266/locations/ | title=Lost in Translation (2003) - Filming & production - IMDb | website=[[IMDb]] }}{{cite web | url=https://tokyofox.net/2022/03/15/kyoto-filming-locations-lost-in-translation-2003/ | title=Kyoto Filming locations: Lost in Translation (2003) | date=15 March 2022 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/kyoto-voice/filming-locations-kyoto/ | title=Filming locations in Kyoto }}{{cite web | url=https://www.focusfeatures.com/article/film-locations_japan_lost-in-translation | title=Finding Japan in Lost in Translation }} == Gallery == {{Gallery |File:Kyoto Nanzenji06n3200.jpg|Hattō |File:Nanzen-in.JPG|Nanzen-in |File:Nanzenji_temple_-_IMG_5429.JPG|Small Hōjō garden |File:Zen_Garden,_Nanzen-ji_Temple_(7005735830)_(3).jpg|Another part of the complex |File:NanzenjiMomiji.jpg|In the fall }} == See also == *[[Ishikawa Goemon]] * [[List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] {{for|the Buddhist temple in Shanxi Province, China|Nanchan Temple}} {{Short description|Zen Buddhist Temple in Kyoto, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Nanzen-ji
南禅寺 | native_name = | image = File:Kyoto_Nanzenji01s5s4272.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = The [[sanmon]] | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 86 Nanzenji Fukuchichō, [[Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|Sakyō-ku]], [[Kyoto]], [[Kyoto Prefecture]] | coordinates = {{coord|35|0|43.14|N|135|47|39.8|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = Nanzen-ji [[Rinzai]] | deity = [[Shaka Nyorai]] (Śākyamuni) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head Temple | website = {{url|http://www.nanzen.net/english/index.html}} | founded_by = [[Emperor Kameyama]] and [[Mukan Fumon]] | year_completed = 1909 (Reconstruction) }} {{nihongo|'''Nanzen-ji'''|南禅寺|''Nanzen-ji''}}, or '''Zuiryusan Nanzen-ji''', formerly {{nihongo|'''Zenrin-ji'''|禅林寺|''Zenrin-ji''}}, is a [[Zen]] [[Buddhism|Buddhist]] [[temple]] in [[Kyoto, Kyoto|Kyoto]], [[Japan]]. [[Emperor Kameyama]] established it in 1291 on the site of his previous detached palace. It is also the headquarters of the Nanzen-ji branch of [[Rinzai Zen]]. The precincts of Nanzen-ji are a [[Cultural Properties of Japan|nationally designated]] [[Monuments of Japan|Historic Site]] and the Hōjō gardens a [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/00003441 |title=南禅寺境内 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1705 |title=南禅寺方丈庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012}} The temple was destroyed in a fire in 1895 and rebuilt in 1909. ==History== Nanzen-ji was founded in the middle [[Kamakura period]] (1291, or [[Shōō (Kamakura period)|Shōō]] 4 in the [[Japanese era name|Japanese era]] system).{{Cite book|title = Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794-1869|last = Ponsonby-Fane|first = Richard|year = 1956|pages = 154|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=9aLpAAAAMAAJ}} It was destroyed by fire in 1393, 1447, and 1467, rebuilt in 1597, and expanded in the Edo era.{{Cite book|title = Kyoto: a Cultural Guide|last = Martin|first = John and Phyllis|publisher = Tuttle Publishing|year = 2013|isbn = 9781462908172|pages = 177}} A large complex, it has varied over time between nine and twelve sub-temples. [[Zenkei Shibayama]], who provided a popular commentary on the [[Mumonkan]], was an abbot of the monastery. == Significance in Zen Buddhism == Nanzen-ji is not itself considered one of the "five great Zen temples of Kyoto"; however, it does play an important role in the "[[Five Mountain System]]" which was modified from Chinese roots. {{Nihongo|[[Tenryū-ji]]|天龍寺|''Tenryū-ji''}} is considered to be one of the so-called {{nihongo|[[Five Mountain System|Kyoto ''Gozan'']]|京都五山|''Kyōto gozan''}} or "five great Zen temples of Kyoto", along with {{nihongo|[[Shōkoku-ji]]|相国寺|''Shōkoku-ji''}}, {{nihongo|[[Kennin-ji]]|建仁寺|''Kennin-ji''}}, {{nihongo|[[Tōfuku-ji]]|東福寺|''Tōfuku-ji''}}, and {{nihongo|[[Manju-ji]]|満寿寺|''Manju-ji''}}. The head temple presiding over the ''Gozan'' in Kyoto is Nanzen-ji.{{Cite book|title = The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Zen Buddhism|last = Baroni|first = Helen Josephine|publisher = The Rosen Publishing Group|year = 2002|isbn = 9780823922406|pages = 116|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=smNM4ElP3XgC}} After the completion of [[Shōkoku-ji]] by [[Ashikaga Yoshimitsu]] in 1386, a new ranking system was created with Nanzen-ji at the top and in a class of its own. Nanzen-ji had the title of "First Temple of The Land" and played a supervising role.{{Cite book|title = Zen Buddhism: A History (Japan)|last = Dumoulin|first = Heinrich|publisher = World Wisdom, Inc.|year = 2005|isbn = 9780941532907|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=hfMkpD_Xr3sC}} {| class="wikitable" | colspan="3" align="center" | Nanzen-ji |- |  !Kyoto !Kamakura |- |First Rank |[[Tenryū-ji]] |[[Kenchō-ji]] |- |Second Rank |[[Shōkoku-ji]] |[[Engaku-ji]] |- |Third Rank |[[Kennin-ji]] |[[Jufuku-ji]] |- |Fourth Rank |[[Tōfuku-ji]] |[[Jōchi-ji]] |- |Fifth Rank |[[Manju-ji]] |[[Jōmyō-ji (Kamakura)|Jōmyō-ji]] |- |} == Notable structures == === Sanmon === [[File:Nanzenji Sanmon 1.jpg|thumb|View from the second story of the Nanzen-ji Sanmon]] The temple's Sanmon gate was originally constructed in the 13th century, destroyed in 1369 at the order of the government, and reconstructed in 1628.{{Cite book|title = Daitokuji: The Visual Cultures of a Zen Monastery|last = Levine|first = Gregory P.A.|publisher = University of Washington Press|year = 2005|isbn = 9780295985404|pages = [https://archive.org/details/daitokujivisualc0000levi/page/94 94]|url = https://archive.org/details/daitokujivisualc0000levi|url-access = registration}} The gate contains stairs to an elevated viewing area, which was the setting for a famous scene in the 1778 [[Kabuki]] play [[Sanmon Gosan no Kiri]]{{Cite book|title = Historical Dictionary of Japanese Traditional Theatre|last = Leiter|first = Samuel L.|publisher = Rowman & Littlefield|year = 2014|isbn = 9781442239111|pages = 476|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=hCkvBQAAQBAJ}} inspired by the story of the criminal [[Ishikawa Goemon]] who is said to have spoken of the beauty of the view (but who was executed prior to the construction of the current gate). === Hōjō === The hōjō (abbot's quarters) of Nanzen-ji is notable both for its gardens and its art. The garden of the hōjō is considered one of the most significant examples of [[Japanese rock garden|karesansui gardens]] (rock gardens), and was built in the 1600s by [[Kobori Masakazu|Kobori Enshu]]. The garden mirrors natural forms, and is seventy percent gravel.{{Cite book|title = Art Of Japanese Gardens|last = Kuck|first = Loraine|publisher = Routledge|year = 2006|isbn = 9781136213625|pages = 230|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=DZ9FAAAAQBAJ}} It has been designated a national Place of Scenic Beauty. The hōjō itself, also known as the Seiryo-den, was given to the temple by the [[Emperor Go-Yōzei]].{{Cite book|title = The Temples of Kyoto|last = Richie|first = Donald|publisher = Tuttle Publishing|year = 2013|isbn = 9781462908578|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=ChTRAgAAQBAJ}} It contains a variety of important screen paintings on gold backgrounds, including two of tigers by [[Kanō Tan'yū]].{{Cite book|title = Of Gardens: Selected Essays|last = Deitz|first = Paula|publisher = University of Pennsylvania Press|year = 2011|isbn = 9780812206968|pages = 301|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=H9xzdCNIXvYC}} It has been designated a National Treasure. {{Gallery |File:Nanzen-ji_Ⅱ.jpg|The Hōjō |File:Nanzen-ji Ⅲ.jpg|The Hōjō garden |File:Hojo,_Konchi-in_-_IMG_5212.JPG|Crane paintings from inside the Hōjō}} === Nanzen-ji Aqueduct === Constructed in 1890 through the temple grounds to carry irrigation water from the [[Lake Biwa Canal]]. {{Gallery |File:Nanzenji aqueduct - IIMG 5411.JPG|Nanzen-ji aqueduct}} ==In media== Parts of the 2003 film ''[[Lost in Translation (film)|Lost in Translation]]'' were filmed there.{{cite web | url=https://www.imdb.com/title/tt0335266/locations/ | title=Lost in Translation (2003) - Filming & production - IMDb | website=[[IMDb]] }}{{cite web | url=https://tokyofox.net/2022/03/15/kyoto-filming-locations-lost-in-translation-2003/ | title=Kyoto Filming locations: Lost in Translation (2003) | date=15 March 2022 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/kyoto-voice/filming-locations-kyoto/ | title=Filming locations in Kyoto }}{{cite web | url=https://www.focusfeatures.com/article/film-locations_japan_lost-in-translation | title=Finding Japan in Lost in Translation }} == Gallery == {{Gallery |File:Kyoto Nanzenji06n3200.jpg|Hattō |File:Nanzen-in.JPG|Nanzen-in |File:Nanzenji_temple_-_IMG_5429.JPG|Small Hōjō garden |File:Zen_Garden,_Nanzen-ji_Temple_(7005735830)_(3).jpg|Another part of the complex |File:NanzenjiMomiji.jpg|In the fall }} == See also == *[[Ishikawa Goemon]] * [[List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]] [[Zenkei Shibayama]], who provided a popular commentary on the [[Mumonkan]], was an abbot of the monastery. == Significance in Zen Buddhism == Nanzen-ji is not itself considered one of the "five great Zen temples of Kyoto"; however, it does play an important role in the "[[Five Mountain System]]" which was modified from Chinese roots. {{Nihongo|[[Tenryū-ji]]|天龍寺|''Tenryū-ji''}} is considered to be one of the so-called {{nihongo|[[Five Mountain System|Kyoto ''Gozan'']]|京都五山|''Kyōto gozan''}} or "five great Zen temples of Kyoto", along with {{nihongo|[[Shōkoku-ji]]|相国寺|''Shōkoku-ji''}}, {{nihongo|[[Kennin-ji]]|建仁寺|''Kennin-ji''}}, {{nihongo|[[Tōfuku-ji]]|東福寺|''Tōfuku-ji''}}, and {{nihongo|[[Manju-ji]]|満寿寺|''Manju-ji''}}. The head temple presiding over the ''Gozan'' in Kyoto is Nanzen-ji.{{Cite book|title = The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Zen Buddhism|last = Baroni|first = Helen Josephine|publisher = The Rosen Publishing Group|year = 2002|isbn = 9780823922406|pages = 116|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=smNM4ElP3XgC}} After the completion of [[Shōkoku-ji]] by [[Ashikaga Yoshimitsu]] in 1386, a new ranking system was created with Nanzen-ji at the top and in a class of its own. Nanzen-ji had the title of "First Temple of The Land" and played a supervising role.{{Cite book|title = Zen Buddhism: A History (Japan)|last = Dumoulin|first = Heinrich|publisher = World Wisdom, Inc.|year = 2005|isbn = 9780941532907|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=hfMkpD_Xr3sC}} {| class="wikitable" | colspan="3" align="center" | Nanzen-ji |- |  !Kyoto !Kamakura |- |First Rank |[[Tenryū-ji]] |[[Kenchō-ji]] |- |Second Rank |[[Shōkoku-ji]] |[[Engaku-ji]] |- |Third Rank |[[Kennin-ji]] |[[Jufuku-ji]] |- |Fourth Rank |[[Tōfuku-ji]] |[[Jōchi-ji]] |- |Fifth Rank |[[Manju-ji]] |[[Jōmyō-ji (Kamakura)|Jōmyō-ji]] |- |} == Notable structures == === Sanmon === [[File:Nanzenji Sanmon 1.jpg|thumb|View from the second story of the Nanzen-ji Sanmon]] The temple's Sanmon gate was originally constructed in the 13th century, destroyed in 1369 at the order of the government, and reconstructed in 1628.{{Cite book|title = Daitokuji: The Visual Cultures of a Zen Monastery|last = Levine|first = Gregory P.A.|publisher = University of Washington Press|year = 2005|isbn = 9780295985404|pages = [https://archive.org/details/daitokujivisualc0000levi/page/94 94]|url = https://archive.org/details/daitokujivisualc0000levi|url-access = registration}} The gate contains stairs to an elevated viewing area, which was the setting for a famous scene in the 1778 [[Kabuki]] play [[Sanmon Gosan no Kiri]]{{Cite book|title = Historical Dictionary of Japanese Traditional Theatre|last = Leiter|first = Samuel L.|publisher = Rowman & Littlefield|year = 2014|isbn = 9781442239111|pages = 476|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=hCkvBQAAQBAJ}} inspired by the story of the criminal [[Ishikawa Goemon]] who is said to have spoken of the beauty of the view (but who was executed prior to the construction of the current gate). === Hōjō === The hōjō (abbot's quarters) of Nanzen-ji is notable both for its gardens and its art. The garden of the hōjō is considered one of the most significant examples of [[Japanese rock garden|karesansui gardens]] (rock gardens), and was built in the 1600s by [[Kobori Masakazu|Kobori Enshu]]. The garden mirrors natural forms, and is seventy percent gravel.{{Cite book|title = Art Of Japanese Gardens|last = Kuck|first = Loraine|publisher = Routledge|year = 2006|isbn = 9781136213625|pages = 230|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=DZ9FAAAAQBAJ}} It has been designated a national Place of Scenic Beauty. The hōjō itself, also known as the Seiryo-den, was given to the temple by the [[Emperor Go-Yōzei]].{{Cite book|title = The Temples of Kyoto|last = Richie|first = Donald|publisher = Tuttle Publishing|year = 2013|isbn = 9781462908578|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=ChTRAgAAQBAJ}} It contains a variety of important screen paintings on gold backgrounds, including two of tigers by [[Kanō Tan'yū]].{{Cite book|title = Of Gardens: Selected Essays|last = Deitz|first = Paula|publisher = University of Pennsylvania Press|year = 2011|isbn = 9780812206968|pages = 301|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=H9xzdCNIXvYC}} It has been designated a National Treasure. {{Gallery |File:Nanzen-ji_Ⅱ.jpg|The Hōjō |File:Nanzen-ji Ⅲ.jpg|The Hōjō garden |File:Hojo,_Konchi-in_-_IMG_5212.JPG|Crane paintings from inside the Hōjō}} === Nanzen-ji Aqueduct === Constructed in 1890 through the temple grounds to carry irrigation water from the [[Lake Biwa Canal]]. {{Gallery |File:Nanzenji aqueduct - IIMG 5411.JPG|Nanzen-ji aqueduct}} ==In media== Parts of the 2003 film ''[[Lost in Translation (film)|Lost in Translation]]'' were filmed there.{{cite web | url=https://www.imdb.com/title/tt0335266/locations/ | title=Lost in Translation (2003) - Filming & production - IMDb | website=[[IMDb]] }}{{cite web | url=https://tokyofox.net/2022/03/15/kyoto-filming-locations-lost-in-translation-2003/ | title=Kyoto Filming locations: Lost in Translation (2003) | date=15 March 2022 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/kyoto-voice/filming-locations-kyoto/ | title=Filming locations in Kyoto }}{{cite web | url=https://www.focusfeatures.com/article/film-locations_japan_lost-in-translation | title=Finding Japan in Lost in Translation }} == Gallery == {{Gallery |File:Kyoto Nanzenji06n3200.jpg|Hattō |File:Nanzen-in.JPG|Nanzen-in |File:Nanzenji_temple_-_IMG_5429.JPG|Small Hōjō garden |File:Zen_Garden,_Nanzen-ji_Temple_(7005735830)_(3).jpg|Another part of the complex |File:NanzenjiMomiji.jpg|In the fall }} == See also == *[[Ishikawa Goemon]] * [[List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)]] *[[List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)]]
