南禅寺 | native_name = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Kyoto_Nanzenji01s5s4272.jpg | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image_size = | alt = | caption = The [[sanmon]] | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 86 Nanzenji Fukuchichō, {{ill|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1321832|lt=Sakyō-ku}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture}} | coordinates = {{coord|35|0|43.14|N|135|47|39.8|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = Nanzen-ji {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai school}} | deity = [[:en:Shaka Nyorai|Shaka Nyorai]] (Shakyamuni Tathāgata) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head Temple | website = {{url|http://www.nanzen.net/english/index.html}} | founded_by = {{ill|Emperor Kameyama|en|Emperor Kameyama|WD=Q360972|lt=Emperor Kameyama}} and [[Mukan Fumon]] | year_completed = 1909 (Reconstruction) }} {{nihongo|'''Nanzen-ji'''|南禅寺|''Nanzen-ji''}}, or '''Zuiryusan Nanzen-ji''', formerly {{nihongo|'''Zenrin-ji'''|禅林寺|''Zenrin-ji''}}, is a {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|WD=Q7953|lt=Zen}} {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhist}} {{ill|Temple|en|Temple|WD=Q44539|lt=Temple}} in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. {{ill|Emperor Kameyama|en|Emperor Kameyama|WD=Q360972|lt=Emperor Kameyama}} established it in 1291 on the site of his previous detached palace. It is also the headquarters of the Nanzen-ji branch of [[:en:Rinzai Zen|Rinzai Zen]]. The precincts of Nanzen-ji are a {{ill|Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q858308|lt=nationally designated}} [[Monuments of Japan|Historic Site]] and the Hōjō gardens a [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/00003441 |title=南禅寺境内 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1705 |title=南禅寺方丈庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012}} The temple was destroyed in a fire in 1895 and rebuilt in 1909. ==History== Nanzen-ji was founded in the middle {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}} (1291, or {{ill|Shōō (Kamakura period)|en|Shōō (Kamakura period)|WD=Q1327371|lt=Shōō}} 4 in the {{ill|Japanese era name|en|Japanese era name|WD=Q24706|lt=Japanese era}} system).{{Cite book|title = Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794-1869|last = Ponsonby-Fane|first = Richard|year = 1956|pages = 154|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=9aLpAAAAMAAJ}} It was destroyed by fire in 1393, 1447, and 1467, rebuilt in 1597, and expanded in the Edo era.{{Cite book|title = Kyoto: a Cultural Guide|last = Martin|first = John and Phyllis|publisher = Tuttle Publishing|year = 2013|isbn = 9781462908172|pages = 177}} A large complex, it has varied over time between nine and twelve sub-temples. {{ill|Zenkei Shibayama|en|Zenkei Shibayama|WD=Q189630|lt=Zenkei Shibayama}}, who provided a popular commentary on the [[:en:Mumonkan|Mumonkan]], was an abbot of the monastery. == Significance in Zen Buddhism == Nanzen-ji is not itself considered one of the "five great Zen temples of Kyoto"; however, it does play an important role in the "{{ill|Five Mountain System|en|Five Mountain System|WD=Q44372|lt=Five Mountain System}}" which was modified from Chinese roots. {{Nihongo|[[Tenryū-ji]]|天龍寺|''Tenryū-ji''}} is considered to be one of the so-called {{nihongo|{{ill|Five Mountain System|en|Five Mountain System|WD=Q44372|lt=Kyoto ''Gozan''}}|京都五山|''Kyōto gozan''}} or "five great Zen temples of Kyoto", along with {{nihongo|{{ill|Shōkoku-ji|en|Shōkoku-ji|WD=Q1786709|lt=Shōkoku-ji}}|相国寺|''Shōkoku-ji''}}, {{nihongo|[[Kennin-ji]]|建仁寺|''Kennin-ji''}}, {{nihongo|[[Tōfuku-ji]]|東福寺|''Tōfuku-ji''}}, and {{nihongo|{{ill|Manju-ji|en|Manju-ji|WD=Q3074317|lt=Manju-ji}}|満寿寺|''Manju-ji''}}. The head temple presiding over the ''Gozan'' in Kyoto is Nanzen-ji.{{Cite book|title = The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Zen Buddhism|last = Baroni|first = Helen Josephine|publisher = The Rosen Publishing Group|year = 2002|isbn = 9780823922406|pages = 116|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=smNM4ElP3XgC}} After the completion of {{ill|Shōkoku-ji|en|Shōkoku-ji|WD=Q1786709|lt=Shōkoku-ji}} by {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|en|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|WD=Q248381|lt=Ashikaga Yoshimitsu}} in 1386, a new Ranking system was created with Nanzen-ji at the top and in a class of its own. Nanzen-ji had the title of "First Temple of The Land" and played a supervising role.{{Cite book|title = Zen Buddhism: A History (Japan)|last = Dumoulin|first = Heinrich|publisher = World Wisdom, Inc.|year = 2005|isbn = 9780941532907|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=hfMkpD_Xr3sC}} {| class="wikitable" | colspan="3" align="center" | Nanzen-ji |- |  !Kyoto !Kamakura |- |First Rank |[[Tenryū-ji]] |[[Kenchō-ji]] |- |Second Rank |{{ill|Shōkoku-ji|en|Shōkoku-ji|WD=Q1786709|lt=Shōkoku-ji}} |[[Engaku-ji]] |- |Third Rank |[[Kennin-ji]] |{{ill|Jufuku-ji|en|Jufuku-ji|WD=Q167087|lt=Jufuku-ji}} |- |Fourth Rank |[[Tōfuku-ji]] |{{ill|Jōchi-ji|en|Jōchi-ji|WD=Q1504539|lt=Jōchi-ji}} |- |Fifth Rank |{{ill|Manju-ji|en|Manju-ji|WD=Q3074317|lt=Manju-ji}} |[[:en:Jōmyō-ji (Kamakura)|Jōmyō-ji]] |- |} == Notable structures == === Sanmon === [[File:Nanzenji Sanmon 1.jpg|thumb|View from the second story of the Nanzen-ji Sanmon]] The temple's Sanmon gate was originally constructed in the 13th century, destroyed in 1369 at the order of the government, and reconstructed in 1628.{{Cite book|title = Daitokuji: The Visual Cultures of a Zen Monastery|last = Levine|first = Gregory P.A.|publisher = University of Washington Press|year = 2005|isbn = 9780295985404|pages = [https://archive.org/details/daitokujivisualc0000levi/page/94 94]|url = https://archive.org/details/daitokujivisualc0000levi|url-access = registration}} The gate contains stairs to an elevated viewing area, which was the setting for a famous scene in the 1778 {{ill|Kabuki|en|Kabuki|WD=Q199701|lt=Kabuki}} play {{ill|Sanmon Gosan no Kiri|en|Sanmon Gosan no Kiri|WD=Q11541534|lt=Sanmon Gosan no Kiri}}{{Cite book|title = Historical Dictionary of Japanese Traditional Theatre|last = Leiter|first = Samuel L.|publisher = Rowman & Littlefield|year = 2014|isbn = 9781442239111|pages = 476|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=hCkvBQAAQBAJ}} inspired by the story of the criminal {{ill|Ishikawa Goemon|en|Ishikawa Goemon|WD=Q707279|lt=Ishikawa Goemon}} who is said to have spoken of the beauty of the view (but who was executed prior to the construction of the current gate). === Hōjō === The hōjō (abbot's quarters) of Nanzen-ji is notable both for its gardens and its art. The garden of the hōjō is considered one of the most significant examples of {{ill|Japanese dry garden|en|Japanese dry garden|WD=Q244226|lt=karesansui gardens}} (rock gardens), and was built in the 1600s by [[:en:Kobori Masakazu|Kobori Enshu]]. The garden mirrors natural forms, and is seventy percent gravel.{{Cite book|title = Art Of Japanese Gardens|last = Kuck|first = Loraine|publisher = Routledge|year = 2006|isbn = 9781136213625|pages = 230|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=DZ9FAAAAQBAJ}} It has been designated a national Place of Scenic Beauty. The hōjō itself, also known as the Seiryo-den, was given to the temple by the {{ill|Emperor Go-Yōzei|en|Emperor Go-Yōzei|WD=Q349440|lt=Emperor Go-Yōzei}}.{{Cite book|title = The Temples of Kyoto|last = Richie|first = Donald|publisher = Tuttle Publishing|year = 2013|isbn = 9781462908578|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=ChTRAgAAQBAJ}} It contains a variety of important screen paintings on gold backgrounds, including two of tigers by {{ill|Kanō Tan'yū|en|Kanō Tan'yū|WD=Q961405|lt=Kanō Tan'yū}}.{{Cite book|title = Of Gardens: Selected Essays|last = Deitz|first = Paula|publisher = University of Pennsylvania Press|year = 2011|isbn = 9780812206968|pages = 301|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=H9xzdCNIXvYC}} It has been designated a National Treasure. {{Gallery |File:Nanzen-ji_Ⅱ.jpg|The Hōjō |File:Nanzen-ji Ⅲ.jpg|The Hōjō garden |File:Hojo,_Konchi-in_-_IMG_5212.JPG|Crane paintings from inside the Hōjō}} === Nanzen-ji Aqueduct === Constructed in 1890 through the temple grounds to carry irrigation water from the {{ill|Lake Biwa Canal|en|Lake Biwa Canal|WD=Q2753954|lt=Lake Biwa Canal}}. {{Gallery |File:Nanzenji aqueduct - IIMG 5411.JPG|Nanzen-ji aqueduct}} ==In media== Parts of the 2003 film ''{{ill|en:Lost in Translation (film)|en|en:Lost in Translation (film)|WD=Q107270|lt=Lost in Translation}}'' were filmed there.{{cite web | url=https://www.imdb.com/title/tt0335266/locations/ | title=Lost in Translation (2003) - Filming & production - IMDb | website=[[IMDb]] }}{{cite web | url=https://tokyofox.net/2022/03/15/kyoto-filming-locations-lost-in-translation-2003/ | title=Kyoto Filming locations: Lost in Translation (2003) | date=15 March 2022 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/kyoto-voice/filming-locations-kyoto/ | title=Filming locations in Kyoto }}{{cite web | url=https://www.focusfeatures.com/article/film-locations_japan_lost-in-translation | title=Finding Japan in Lost in Translation }} == Gallery == {{Gallery |File:Kyoto Nanzenji06n3200.jpg|Hattō |File:Nanzen-in.JPG|Nanzen-in |File:Nanzenji_temple_-_IMG_5429.JPG|Small Hōjō garden |File:Zen_Garden,_Nanzen-ji_Temple_(7005735830)_(3).jpg|Another part of the complex |File:NanzenjiMomiji.jpg|In the fall }} == See also == *{{ill|Ishikawa Goemon|en|Ishikawa Goemon|WD=Q707279|lt=Ishikawa Goemon}} * [[:en:List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto|List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] *{{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|WD=Q3245523|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)}} *{{ill|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)|en|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)|WD=Q7883225|lt=en:List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)}} ==References== {{Reflist|2}} ==Further reading== *{{cite book |last= Kawaguchi |first= Yoko |title= Japanese Zen Gardens |location= London |publisher= Francis Lincoln |date= 2014 |isbn= 978-0-7112-3447-5}} == External links == * {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category-inline|Nanzen-ji}} * [https://web.archive.org/web/20081217034816/http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/ Kyoto Prefectural Tourism Guide]: [https://web.archive.org/web/20081221164657/http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/temples/m_nanzenji/ Nanzen-ji] * [http://zen.rinnou.net/index.html Joint Council for Japanese Rinzai and Obaku Zen]: [http://zen.rinnou.net/head_temples/03nanzen.html Nanzen-ji] * [http://www.sacred-destinations.com/japan/kyoto.htm Sacred Destinations]: 南禅寺 | native_name = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Kyoto_Nanzenji01s5s4272.jpg | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image_size = | alt = | caption = The [[sanmon]] | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 86 Nanzenji Fukuchichō, {{ill|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1321832|lt=Sakyō-ku}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture}} | coordinates = {{coord|35|0|43.14|N|135|47|39.8|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = Nanzen-ji {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai school}} | deity = [[:en:Shaka Nyorai|Shaka Nyorai]] (Shakyamuni Tathāgata) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head Temple | website = {{url|http://www.nanzen.net/english/index.html}} | founded_by = {{ill|Emperor Kameyama|en|Emperor Kameyama|WD=Q360972|lt=Emperor Kameyama}} and [[Mukan Fumon]] | year_completed = 1909 (Reconstruction) }} {{nihongo|'''Nanzen-ji'''|南禅寺|''Nanzen-ji''}}, or '''Zuiryusan Nanzen-ji''', formerly {{nihongo|'''Zenrin-ji'''|禅林寺|''Zenrin-ji''}}, is a {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|WD=Q7953|lt=Zen}} {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhist}} {{ill|Temple|en|Temple|WD=Q44539|lt=Temple}} in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. {{ill|Emperor Kameyama|en|Emperor Kameyama|WD=Q360972|lt=Emperor Kameyama}} established it in 1291 on the site of his previous detached palace. It is also the headquarters of the Nanzen-ji branch of [[:en:Rinzai Zen|Rinzai Zen]]. The precincts of Nanzen-ji are a {{ill|Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q858308|lt=nationally designated}} [[Monuments of Japan|Historic Site]] and the Hōjō gardens a [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/00003441 |title=南禅寺境内 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1705 |title=南禅寺方丈庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012}} The temple was destroyed in a fire in 1895 and rebuilt in 1909. ==History== Nanzen-ji was founded in the middle {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}} (1291, or {{ill|Shōō (Kamakura period)|en|Shōō (Kamakura period)|WD=Q1327371|lt=Shōō}} 4 in the {{ill|Japanese era name|en|Japanese era name|WD=Q24706|lt=Japanese era}} system).{{Cite book|title = Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794-1869|last = Ponsonby-Fane|first = Richard|year = 1956|pages = 154|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=9aLpAAAAMAAJ}} It was destroyed by fire in 1393, 1447, and 1467, rebuilt in 1597, and expanded in the Edo era.{{Cite book|title = Kyoto: a Cultural Guide|last = Martin|first = John and Phyllis|publisher = Tuttle Publishing|year = 2013|isbn = 9781462908172|pages = 177}} A large complex, it has varied over time between nine and twelve sub-temples. {{ill|Zenkei Shibayama|en|Zenkei Shibayama|WD=Q189630|lt=Zenkei Shibayama}}, who provided a popular commentary on the [[:en:Mumonkan|Mumonkan]], was an abbot of the monastery. == Significance in Zen Buddhism == Nanzen-ji is not itself considered one of the "five great Zen temples of Kyoto"; however, it does play an important role in the "{{ill|Five Mountain System|en|Five Mountain System|WD=Q44372|lt=Five Mountain System}}" which was modified from Chinese roots. {{Nihongo|[[Tenryū-ji]]|天龍寺|''Tenryū-ji''}} is considered to be one of the so-called {{nihongo|{{ill|Five Mountain System|en|Five Mountain System|WD=Q44372|lt=Kyoto ''Gozan''}}|京都五山|''Kyōto gozan''}} or "five great Zen temples of Kyoto", along with {{nihongo|{{ill|Shōkoku-ji|en|Shōkoku-ji|WD=Q1786709|lt=Shōkoku-ji}}|相国寺|''Shōkoku-ji''}}, {{nihongo|[[Kennin-ji]]|建仁寺|''Kennin-ji''}}, {{nihongo|[[Tōfuku-ji]]|東福寺|''Tōfuku-ji''}}, and {{nihongo|{{ill|Manju-ji|en|Manju-ji|WD=Q3074317|lt=Manju-ji}}|満寿寺|''Manju-ji''}}. The head temple presiding over the ''Gozan'' in Kyoto is Nanzen-ji.{{Cite book|title = The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Zen Buddhism|last = Baroni|first = Helen Josephine|publisher = The Rosen Publishing Group|year = 2002|isbn = 9780823922406|pages = 116|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=smNM4ElP3XgC}} After the completion of {{ill|Shōkoku-ji|en|Shōkoku-ji|WD=Q1786709|lt=Shōkoku-ji}} by {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|en|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|WD=Q248381|lt=Ashikaga Yoshimitsu}} in 1386, a new Ranking system was created with Nanzen-ji at the top and in a class of its own. Nanzen-ji had the title of "First Temple of The Land" and played a supervising role.{{Cite book|title = Zen Buddhism: A History (Japan)|last = Dumoulin|first = Heinrich|publisher = World Wisdom, Inc.|year = 2005|isbn = 9780941532907|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=hfMkpD_Xr3sC}} {| class="wikitable" | colspan="3" align="center" | Nanzen-ji |- |  !Kyoto !Kamakura |- |First Rank |[[Tenryū-ji]] |[[Kenchō-ji]] |- |Second Rank |{{ill|Shōkoku-ji|en|Shōkoku-ji|WD=Q1786709|lt=Shōkoku-ji}} |[[Engaku-ji]] |- |Third Rank |[[Kennin-ji]] |{{ill|Jufuku-ji|en|Jufuku-ji|WD=Q167087|lt=Jufuku-ji}} |- |Fourth Rank |[[Tōfuku-ji]] |{{ill|Jōchi-ji|en|Jōchi-ji|WD=Q1504539|lt=Jōchi-ji}} |- |Fifth Rank |{{ill|Manju-ji|en|Manju-ji|WD=Q3074317|lt=Manju-ji}} |[[:en:Jōmyō-ji (Kamakura)|Jōmyō-ji]] |- |} == Notable structures == === Sanmon === [[File:Nanzenji Sanmon 1.jpg|thumb|View from the second story of the Nanzen-ji Sanmon]] The temple's Sanmon gate was originally constructed in the 13th century, destroyed in 1369 at the order of the government, and reconstructed in 1628.{{Cite book|title = Daitokuji: The Visual Cultures of a Zen Monastery|last = Levine|first = Gregory P.A.|publisher = University of Washington Press|year = 2005|isbn = 9780295985404|pages = [https://archive.org/details/daitokujivisualc0000levi/page/94 94]|url = https://archive.org/details/daitokujivisualc0000levi|url-access = registration}} The gate contains stairs to an elevated viewing area, which was the setting for a famous scene in the 1778 {{ill|Kabuki|en|Kabuki|WD=Q199701|lt=Kabuki}} play {{ill|Sanmon Gosan no Kiri|en|Sanmon Gosan no Kiri|WD=Q11541534|lt=Sanmon Gosan no Kiri}}{{Cite book|title = Historical Dictionary of Japanese Traditional Theatre|last = Leiter|first = Samuel L.|publisher = Rowman & Littlefield|year = 2014|isbn = 9781442239111|pages = 476|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=hCkvBQAAQBAJ}} inspired by the story of the criminal {{ill|Ishikawa Goemon|en|Ishikawa Goemon|WD=Q707279|lt=Ishikawa Goemon}} who is said to have spoken of the beauty of the view (but who was executed prior to the construction of the current gate). === Hōjō === The hōjō (abbot's quarters) of Nanzen-ji is notable both for its gardens and its art. The garden of the hōjō is considered one of the most significant examples of {{ill|Japanese dry garden|en|Japanese dry garden|WD=Q244226|lt=karesansui gardens}} (rock gardens), and was built in the 1600s by [[:en:Kobori Masakazu|Kobori Enshu]]. The garden mirrors natural forms, and is seventy percent gravel.{{Cite book|title = Art Of Japanese Gardens|last = Kuck|first = Loraine|publisher = Routledge|year = 2006|isbn = 9781136213625|pages = 230|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=DZ9FAAAAQBAJ}} It has been designated a national Place of Scenic Beauty. The hōjō itself, also known as the Seiryo-den, was given to the temple by the {{ill|Emperor Go-Yōzei|en|Emperor Go-Yōzei|WD=Q349440|lt=Emperor Go-Yōzei}}.{{Cite book|title = The Temples of Kyoto|last = Richie|first = Donald|publisher = Tuttle Publishing|year = 2013|isbn = 9781462908578|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=ChTRAgAAQBAJ}} It contains a variety of important screen paintings on gold backgrounds, including two of tigers by {{ill|Kanō Tan'yū|en|Kanō Tan'yū|WD=Q961405|lt=Kanō Tan'yū}}.{{Cite book|title = Of Gardens: Selected Essays|last = Deitz|first = Paula|publisher = University of Pennsylvania Press|year = 2011|isbn = 9780812206968|pages = 301|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=H9xzdCNIXvYC}} It has been designated a National Treasure. {{Gallery |File:Nanzen-ji_Ⅱ.jpg|The Hōjō |File:Nanzen-ji Ⅲ.jpg|The Hōjō garden |File:Hojo,_Konchi-in_-_IMG_5212.JPG|Crane paintings from inside the Hōjō}} === Nanzen-ji Aqueduct === Constructed in 1890 through the temple grounds to carry irrigation water from the {{ill|Lake Biwa Canal|en|Lake Biwa Canal|WD=Q2753954|lt=Lake Biwa Canal}}. {{Gallery |File:Nanzenji aqueduct - IIMG 5411.JPG|Nanzen-ji aqueduct}} ==In media== Parts of the 2003 film ''{{ill|en:Lost in Translation (film)|en|en:Lost in Translation (film)|WD=Q107270|lt=Lost in Translation}}'' were filmed there.{{cite web | url=https://www.imdb.com/title/tt0335266/locations/ | title=Lost in Translation (2003) - Filming & production - IMDb | website=[[IMDb]] }}{{cite web | url=https://tokyofox.net/2022/03/15/kyoto-filming-locations-lost-in-translation-2003/ | title=Kyoto Filming locations: Lost in Translation (2003) | date=15 March 2022 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/kyoto-voice/filming-locations-kyoto/ | title=Filming locations in Kyoto }}{{cite web | url=https://www.focusfeatures.com/article/film-locations_japan_lost-in-translation | title=Finding Japan in Lost in Translation }} == Gallery == {{Gallery |File:Kyoto Nanzenji06n3200.jpg|Hattō |File:Nanzen-in.JPG|Nanzen-in |File:Nanzenji_temple_-_IMG_5429.JPG|Small Hōjō garden |File:Zen_Garden,_Nanzen-ji_Temple_(7005735830)_(3).jpg|Another part of the complex |File:NanzenjiMomiji.jpg|In the fall }} == See also == *{{ill|Ishikawa Goemon|en|Ishikawa Goemon|WD=Q707279|lt=Ishikawa Goemon}} * [[:en:List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto|List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] *{{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|WD=Q3245523|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)}} *{{ill|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)|en|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)|WD=Q7883225|lt=en:List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)}} ==References== {{Reflist|2}} ==Further reading== *{{cite book |last= Kawaguchi |first= Yoko |title= Japanese Zen Gardens |location= London |publisher= Francis Lincoln |date= 2014 |isbn= 978-0-7112-3447-5}} == External links == * {{Commons [https://web.archive.org/web/20081221164657/http://www.pref.kyoto.jp/visitkyoto/en/theme/sites/shrines/temples/m_nanzenji/ Nanzen-ji] * [http://zen.rinnou.net/index.html Joint Council for Japanese 南禅寺 | native_name = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Kyoto_Nanzenji01s5s4272.jpg | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image_size = | alt = | caption = The [[sanmon]] | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 86 Nanzenji Fukuchichō, {{ill|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1321832|lt=Sakyō-ku}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture}} | coordinates = {{coord|35|0|43.14|N|135|47|39.8|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = Nanzen-ji {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai school}} | deity = [[:en:Shaka Nyorai|Shaka Nyorai]] (Shakyamuni Tathāgata) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head Temple | website = {{url|http://www.nanzen.net/english/index.html}} | founded_by = {{ill|Emperor Kameyama|en|Emperor Kameyama|WD=Q360972|lt=Emperor Kameyama}} and [[Mukan Fumon]] | year_completed = 1909 (Reconstruction) }} {{nihongo|'''Nanzen-ji'''|南禅寺|''Nanzen-ji''}}, or '''Zuiryusan Nanzen-ji''', formerly {{nihongo|'''Zenrin-ji'''|禅林寺|''Zenrin-ji''}}, is a {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|WD=Q7953|lt=Zen}} {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhist}} {{ill|Temple|en|Temple|WD=Q44539|lt=Temple}} in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. {{ill|Emperor Kameyama|en|Emperor Kameyama|WD=Q360972|lt=Emperor Kameyama}} established it in 1291 on the site of his previous detached palace. It is also the headquarters of the Nanzen-ji branch of [[:en:Rinzai Zen|Rinzai Zen]]. The precincts of Nanzen-ji are a {{ill|Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q858308|lt=nationally designated}} [[Monuments of Japan|Historic Site]] and the Hōjō gardens a [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/00003441 |title=南禅寺境内 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1705 |title=南禅寺方丈庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012}} The temple was destroyed in a fire in 1895 and rebuilt in 1909. ==History== Nanzen-ji was founded in the middle {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period}} (1291, or {{ill|Shōō (Kamakura period)|en|Shōō (Kamakura period)|WD=Q1327371|lt=Shōō}} 4 in the {{ill|Japanese era name|en|Japanese era name|WD=Q24706|lt=Japanese era}} system).{{Cite book|title = Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794-1869|last = Ponsonby-Fane|first = Richard|year = 1956|pages = 154|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=9aLpAAAAMAAJ}} It was destroyed by fire in 1393, 1447, and 1467, rebuilt in 1597, and expanded in the Edo era.{{Cite book|title = Kyoto: a Cultural Guide|last = Martin|first = John and Phyllis|publisher = Tuttle Publishing|year = 2013|isbn = 9781462908172|pages = 177}} A large complex, it has varied over time between nine and twelve sub-temples. {{ill|Zenkei Shibayama|en|Zenkei Shibayama|WD=Q189630|lt=Zenkei Shibayama}}, who provided a popular commentary on the [[:en:Mumonkan|Mumonkan]], was an abbot of the monastery. == Significance in Zen Buddhism == Nanzen-ji is not itself considered one of the "five great Zen temples of Kyoto"; however, it does play an important role in the "{{ill|Five Mountain System|en|Five Mountain System|WD=Q44372|lt=Five Mountain System}}" which was modified from Chinese roots. {{Nihongo|[[Tenryū-ji]]|天龍寺|''Tenryū-ji''}} is considered to be one of the so-called {{nihongo|{{ill|Five Mountain System|en|Five Mountain System|WD=Q44372|lt=Kyoto ''Gozan''}}|京都五山|''Kyōto gozan''}} or "five great Zen temples of Kyoto", along with {{nihongo|{{ill|Shōkoku-ji|en|Shōkoku-ji|WD=Q1786709|lt=Shōkoku-ji}}|相国寺|''Shōkoku-ji''}}, {{nihongo|[[Kennin-ji]]|建仁寺|''Kennin-ji''}}, {{nihongo|[[Tōfuku-ji]]|東福寺|''Tōfuku-ji''}}, and {{nihongo|{{ill|Manju-ji|en|Manju-ji|WD=Q3074317|lt=Manju-ji}}|満寿寺|''Manju-ji''}}. The head temple presiding over the ''Gozan'' in Kyoto is Nanzen-ji.{{Cite book|title = The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Zen Buddhism|last = Baroni|first = Helen Josephine|publisher = The Rosen Publishing Group|year = 2002|isbn = 9780823922406|pages = 116|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=smNM4ElP3XgC}} After the completion of {{ill|Shōkoku-ji|en|Shōkoku-ji|WD=Q1786709|lt=Shōkoku-ji}} by {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|en|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|WD=Q248381|lt=Ashikaga Yoshimitsu}} in 1386, a new Ranking system was created with Nanzen-ji at the top and in a class of its own. Nanzen-ji had the title of "First Temple of The Land" and played a supervising role.{{Cite book|title = Zen Buddhism: A History (Japan)|last = Dumoulin|first = Heinrich|publisher = World Wisdom, |File:Nanzen-ji_Ⅱ.jpg|The Hōjō |File:Nanzen-ji Ⅲ.jpg|The Hōjō garden |File:Hojo,_Konchi-in_-_IMG_5212.JPG|Crane paintings from inside the Hōjō}} === Nanzen-ji Aqueduct === Constructed in 1890 through [[arz:نانزين جى]][[cs:Nanzendži]][[de:Nanzen-ji]][[en:Nanzen-ji]][[es:Nanzen-ji]][[fa:نانزن-جی]][[fi:Nanzen-ji]][[fr:Nanzen-ji]][[gan:南禪寺]][[hu:Nanzendzsi]][[it:Nanzen-ji]][[ja:南禅寺]][[ko:난젠지]][[my:နန်းဇန်းဂျိ]][[nl:Nanzen-ji]][[pl:Nanzen-ji]][[ro:Nanzen-ji]][[ru:Нандзэн-дзи]][[th:นันเซ็นจิ]][[tr:Nanzen-ji]][[zh:南禪寺 (京都市)]] [[arz:نانزين جى]][[cs:Nanzendži]][[de:Nanzen-ji]][[en:Nanzen-ji]][[es:Nanzen-ji]][[fa:نانزن-جی]][[fi:Nanzen-ji]][[fr:Nanzen-ji]][[gan:南禪寺]][[hu:Nanzendzsi]][[it:Nanzen-ji]][[ja:南禅寺]][[ko:난젠지]][[my:နန်းဇန်းဂျိ]][[nl:Nanzen-ji]][[pl:Nanzen-ji]][[ro:Nanzen-ji]][[ru:Нандзэн-дзи]][[th:นันเซ็นจิ]][[tr:Nanzen-ji]][[zh:南禪寺 (京都市)]] [[arz:نانزين جى]][[cs:Nanzendži]][[de:Nanzen-ji]][[en:Nanzen-ji]][[es:Nanzen-ji]][[fa:نانزن-جی]][[fi:Nanzen-ji]][[fr:Nanzen-ji]][[gan:南禪寺]][[hu:Nanzendzsi]][[it:Nanzen-ji]][[ja:南禅寺]][[ko:난젠지]][[my:နန်းဇန်းဂျိ]][[nl:Nanzen-ji]][[pl:Nanzen-ji]][[ro:Nanzen-ji]][[ru:Нандзэн-дзи]][[th:นันเซ็นจิ]][[tr:Nanzen-ji]][[zh:南禪寺 (京都市)]] {{Infobox Buddhist temple|image=File:Kyoto Nanzenji01s5s4272.jpg|religious_affiliation={{ill|Nanzen-ji school|WD=Q126509708|1=Nanzen-ji school}}|prefecture={{ill|Sakyō-ku|WD=Q1321832|1=Sakyō-ku, Kyoto}}|coordinates={{Coord|35.011111111111|N|135.79416666667|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|founded_by={{ill|Emperor Kameyama|WD=Q360972|1=Emperor Kameyama}}; {{ill|Kian Soen|WD=Q11630267|1=Kian Soen}}; {{ill|Mukan Fumon|WD=Q11085497|1=Mukan Fumon}}|deity={{ill|Shakyamuni Tathāgata|WD=Q11644537|1=Shakyamuni Tathāgata}}|part_of={{ill|Kyoto Gozan|WD=Q8193432|1=Kyoto Gozan}}}} {{for|the Buddhist temple in Shanxi Province, China|Nanchan Temple}} {{Short description|Zen Buddhist Temple in Kyoto, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Nanzen-ji
南禅寺 | native_name = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Kyoto_Nanzenji01s5s4272.jpg | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image_size = | alt = | caption = The [[sanmon]] | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 86 Nanzenji Fukuchichō, {{ill|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1321832|lt=Sakyō-ku|1=Sakyō-ku, Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}} | coordinates = {{coord|35|0|43.14|N|135|47|39.8|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = Nanzen-ji {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai school|1=Rinzai school}} | deity = [[:en:Shaka Nyorai|Shaka Nyorai]] (Shakyamuni Tathāgata) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head Temple | website = {{url|http://www.nanzen.net/english/index.html}} | founded_by = {{ill|Emperor Kameyama|en|Emperor Kameyama|WD=Q360972|lt=Emperor Kameyama|1=Emperor Kameyama}} and [[Mukan Fumon]] | year_completed = 1909 (Reconstruction) }} {{nihongo|'''Nanzen-ji'''|南禅寺|''Nanzen-ji''}}, or '''Zuiryusan Nanzen-ji''', formerly {{nihongo|'''Zenrin-ji'''|禅林寺|''Zenrin-ji''}}, is a {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|WD=Q7953|lt=Zen|1=Zen}} {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhist|1=Buddhism}} {{ill|Temple|en|Temple|WD=Q44539|lt=Temple|1=Temple}} in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. {{ill|Emperor Kameyama|en|Emperor Kameyama|WD=Q360972|lt=Emperor Kameyama|1=Emperor Kameyama}} established it in 1291 on the site of his previous detached palace. It is also the headquarters of the Nanzen-ji branch of [[:en:Rinzai Zen|Rinzai Zen]]. The precincts of Nanzen-ji are a {{ill|Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q858308|lt=nationally designated|1=Cultural Property (Japan)}} [[Monuments of Japan|Historic Site]] and the Hōjō gardens a [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/00003441 |title=南禅寺境内 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1705 |title=南禅寺方丈庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012}} The temple was destroyed in a fire in 1895 and rebuilt in 1909. ==History== Nanzen-ji was founded in the middle {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}} (1291, or {{ill|Shōō (Kamakura period)|en|Shōō (Kamakura period)|WD=Q1327371|lt=Shōō|1=Shōō (Kamakura period)}} 4 in the {{ill|Japanese era name|en|Japanese era name|WD=Q24706|lt=Japanese era|1=Japanese era name}} system).{{Cite book|title = Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794-1869|last = Ponsonby-Fane|first = Richard|year = 1956|pages = 154|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=9aLpAAAAMAAJ}} It was destroyed by fire in 1393, 1447, and 1467, rebuilt in 1597, and expanded in the Edo era.{{Cite book|title = Kyoto: a Cultural Guide|last = Martin|first = John and Phyllis|publisher = Tuttle Publishing|year = 2013|isbn = 9781462908172|pages = 177}} A large complex, it has varied over time between nine and twelve sub-temples. {{ill|Zenkei Shibayama|en|Zenkei Shibayama|WD=Q189630|lt=Zenkei Shibayama|1=Zenkei Shibayama}}, who provided a popular commentary on the [[:en:Mumonkan|Mumonkan]], was an abbot of the monastery. == Significance in Zen Buddhism == Nanzen-ji is not itself considered one of the "five great Zen temples of Kyoto"; however, it does play an important role in the "{{ill|Five Mountain System|en|Five Mountain System|WD=Q44372|lt=Five Mountain System|1=Five Mountain System}}" which was modified from Chinese roots. {{Nihongo|[[Tenryū-ji]]|天龍寺|''Tenryū-ji''}} is considered to be one of the so-called {{nihongo|{{ill|Five Mountain System|en|Five Mountain System|WD=Q44372|lt=Kyoto ''Gozan''|1=Five Mountain System}}|京都五山|''Kyōto gozan''}} or "five great Zen temples of Kyoto", along with {{nihongo|{{ill|Shōkoku-ji|en|Shōkoku-ji|WD=Q1786709|lt=Shōkoku-ji|1=Shōkoku-ji}}|相国寺|''Shōkoku-ji''}}, {{nihongo|[[Kennin-ji]]|建仁寺|''Kennin-ji''}}, {{nihongo|[[Tōfuku-ji]]|東福寺|''Tōfuku-ji''}}, and {{nihongo|{{ill|Manju-ji|en|Manju-ji|WD=Q3074317|lt=Manju-ji|1=Manju-ji}}|満寿寺|''Manju-ji''}}. The head temple presiding over the ''Gozan'' in Kyoto is Nanzen-ji.{{Cite book|title = The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Zen Buddhism|last = Baroni|first = Helen Josephine|publisher = The Rosen Publishing Group|year = 2002|isbn = 9780823922406|pages = 116|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=smNM4ElP3XgC}} After the completion of {{ill|Shōkoku-ji|en|Shōkoku-ji|WD=Q1786709|lt=Shōkoku-ji|1=Shōkoku-ji}} by {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|en|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|WD=Q248381|lt=Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|1=Ashikaga Yoshimitsu}} in 1386, a new Ranking system was created with Nanzen-ji at the top and in a class of its own. Nanzen-ji had the title of "First Temple of The Land" and played a supervising role.{{Cite book|title = Zen Buddhism: A History (Japan)|last = Dumoulin|first = Heinrich|publisher = World Wisdom, Inc.|year = 2005|isbn = 9780941532907|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=hfMkpD_Xr3sC}} {| class="wikitable" | colspan="3" align="center" | Nanzen-ji |- |  !Kyoto !Kamakura |- |First Rank |[[Tenryū-ji]] |[[Kenchō-ji]] |- |Second Rank |{{ill|Shōkoku-ji|en|Shōkoku-ji|WD=Q1786709|lt=Shōkoku-ji|1=Shōkoku-ji}} |[[Engaku-ji]] |- |Third Rank |[[Kennin-ji]] |{{ill|Jufuku-ji|en|Jufuku-ji|WD=Q167087|lt=Jufuku-ji|1=Jufuku-ji}} |- |Fourth Rank |[[Tōfuku-ji]] |{{ill|Jōchi-ji|en|Jōchi-ji|WD=Q1504539|lt=Jōchi-ji|1=Jōchi-ji}} |- |Fifth Rank |{{ill|Manju-ji|en|Manju-ji|WD=Q3074317|lt=Manju-ji|1=Manju-ji}} |[[:en:Jōmyō-ji (Kamakura)|Jōmyō-ji]] |- |} == Notable structures == === Sanmon === [[File:Nanzenji Sanmon 1.jpg|thumb|View from the second story of the Nanzen-ji Sanmon]] The temple's Sanmon gate was originally constructed in the 13th century, destroyed in 1369 at the order of the government, and reconstructed in 1628.{{Cite book|title = Daitokuji: The Visual Cultures of a Zen Monastery|last = Levine|first = Gregory P.A.|publisher = University of Washington Press|year = 2005|isbn = 9780295985404|pages = [https://archive.org/details/daitokujivisualc0000levi/page/94 94]|url = https://archive.org/details/daitokujivisualc0000levi|url-access = registration}} The gate contains stairs to an elevated viewing area, which was the setting for a famous scene in the 1778 {{ill|Kabuki|en|Kabuki|WD=Q199701|lt=Kabuki|1=Kabuki}} play {{ill|Sanmon Gosan no Kiri|en|Sanmon Gosan no Kiri|WD=Q11541534|lt=Sanmon Gosan no Kiri|1=Sanmon Gosan no Kiri}}{{Cite book|title = Historical Dictionary of Japanese Traditional Theatre|last = Leiter|first = Samuel L.|publisher = Rowman & Littlefield|year = 2014|isbn = 9781442239111|pages = 476|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=hCkvBQAAQBAJ}} inspired by the story of the criminal {{ill|Ishikawa Goemon|en|Ishikawa Goemon|WD=Q707279|lt=Ishikawa Goemon|1=Ishikawa Goemon}} who is said to have spoken of the beauty of the view (but who was executed prior to the construction of the current gate). === Hōjō === The hōjō (abbot's quarters) of Nanzen-ji is notable both for its gardens and its art. The garden of the hōjō is considered one of the most significant examples of {{ill|Japanese dry garden|en|Japanese dry garden|WD=Q244226|lt=karesansui gardens|1=Japanese dry garden}} (rock gardens), and was built in the 1600s by [[:en:Kobori Masakazu|Kobori Enshu]]. The garden mirrors natural forms, and is seventy percent gravel.{{Cite book|title = Art Of Japanese Gardens|last = Kuck|first = Loraine|publisher = Routledge|year = 2006|isbn = 9781136213625|pages = 230|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=DZ9FAAAAQBAJ}} It has been designated a national Place of Scenic Beauty. The hōjō itself, also known as the Seiryo-den, was given to the temple by the {{ill|Emperor Go-Yōzei|en|Emperor Go-Yōzei|WD=Q349440|lt=Emperor Go-Yōzei|1=Emperor Go-Yōzei}}.{{Cite book|title = The Temples of Kyoto|last = Richie|first = Donald|publisher = Tuttle Publishing|year = 2013|isbn = 9781462908578|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=ChTRAgAAQBAJ}} It contains a variety of important screen paintings on gold backgrounds, including two of tigers by {{ill|Kanō Tan'yū|en|Kanō Tan'yū|WD=Q961405|lt=Kanō Tan'yū|1=Kanō Tan'yū}}.{{Cite book|title = Of Gardens: Selected Essays|last = Deitz|first = Paula|publisher = University of Pennsylvania Press|year = 2011|isbn = 9780812206968|pages = 301|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=H9xzdCNIXvYC}} It has been designated a National Treasure. {{Gallery |File:Nanzen-ji_Ⅱ.jpg|The Hōjō |File:Nanzen-ji Ⅲ.jpg|The Hōjō garden |File:Hojo,_Konchi-in_-_IMG_5212.JPG|Crane paintings from inside the Hōjō}} === Nanzen-ji Aqueduct === Constructed in 1890 through the temple grounds to carry irrigation water from the {{ill|Lake Biwa Canal|en|Lake Biwa Canal|WD=Q2753954|lt=Lake Biwa Canal|1=Lake Biwa Canal}}. {{Gallery |File:Nanzenji aqueduct - IIMG 5411.JPG|Nanzen-ji aqueduct}} ==In media== Parts of the 2003 film ''{{ill|en:Lost in Translation (film)|en|en:Lost in Translation (film)|WD=Q107270|lt=Lost in Translation|1=Lost in Translation (film)}}'' were filmed there.{{cite web | url=https://www.imdb.com/title/tt0335266/locations/ | title=Lost in Translation (2003) - Filming & production - IMDb | website=[[IMDb]] }}{{cite web | url=https://tokyofox.net/2022/03/15/kyoto-filming-locations-lost-in-translation-2003/ | title=Kyoto Filming locations: Lost in Translation (2003) | date=15 March 2022 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/kyoto-voice/filming-locations-kyoto/ | title=Filming locations in Kyoto }}{{cite web | url=https://www.focusfeatures.com/article/film-locations_japan_lost-in-translation | title=Finding Japan in Lost in Translation }} == Gallery == {{Gallery |File:Kyoto Nanzenji06n3200.jpg|Hattō |File:Nanzen-in.JPG|Nanzen-in |File:Nanzenji_temple_-_IMG_5429.JPG|Small Hōjō garden |File:Zen_Garden,_Nanzen-ji_Temple_(7005735830)_(3).jpg|Another part of the complex |File:NanzenjiMomiji.jpg|In the fall }} == See also == *{{ill|Ishikawa Goemon|en|Ishikawa Goemon|WD=Q707279|lt=Ishikawa Goemon|1=Ishikawa Goemon}} * [[:en:List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto|List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] *{{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|WD=Q3245523|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)}} *{{ill|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)|en|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)|WD=Q7883225|lt=en:List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)}} ==References== {{Reflist|2}} ==Further reading== *{{cite book |last= Kawaguchi |first= Yoko |title= Japanese Zen Gardens |location= London |publisher= Francis Lincoln |date= 2014 |isbn= 978-0-7112-3447-5}} == External links == * {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category-inline|Nanzen-ji}} * Rinzai and Obaku Zen]: [http://zen.rinnou.net/head_temples/03nanzen.html Nanzen-ji] * [http://www.sacred-destinations.com/japan/kyoto.htm Sacred Destinations]: 南禅寺 | native_name = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Kyoto_Nanzenji01s5s4272.jpg | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image_size = | alt = | caption = The [[sanmon]] | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 86 Nanzenji Fukuchichō, {{ill|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1321832|lt=Sakyō-ku|1=Sakyō-ku, Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}} | coordinates = {{coord|35|0|43.14|N|135|47|39.8|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = Nanzen-ji {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai school|1=Rinzai school}} | deity = [[:en:Shaka Nyorai|Shaka Nyorai]] (Shakyamuni Tathāgata) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head Temple | website = {{url|http://www.nanzen.net/english/index.html}} | founded_by = {{ill|Emperor Kameyama|en|Emperor Kameyama|WD=Q360972|lt=Emperor Kameyama|1=Emperor Kameyama}} and [[Mukan Fumon]] | year_completed = 1909 (Reconstruction) }} {{nihongo|'''Nanzen-ji'''|南禅寺|''Nanzen-ji''}}, or '''Zuiryusan Nanzen-ji''', formerly {{nihongo|'''Zenrin-ji'''|禅林寺|''Zenrin-ji''}}, is a {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|WD=Q7953|lt=Zen|1=Zen}} {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhist|1=Buddhism}} {{ill|Temple|en|Temple|WD=Q44539|lt=Temple|1=Temple}} in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. {{ill|Emperor Kameyama|en|Emperor Kameyama|WD=Q360972|lt=Emperor Kameyama|1=Emperor Kameyama}} established it in 1291 on the site of his previous detached palace. It is also the headquarters of the Nanzen-ji branch of [[:en:Rinzai Zen|Rinzai Zen]]. The precincts of Nanzen-ji are a {{ill|Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q858308|lt=nationally designated|1=Cultural Property (Japan)}} [[Monuments of Japan|Historic Site]] and the Hōjō gardens a [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/00003441 |title=南禅寺境内 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1705 |title=南禅寺方丈庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012}} The temple was destroyed in a fire in 1895 and rebuilt in 1909. ==History== Nanzen-ji was founded in the middle {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}} (1291, or {{ill|Shōō (Kamakura period)|en|Shōō (Kamakura period)|WD=Q1327371|lt=Shōō|1=Shōō (Kamakura period)}} 4 in the {{ill|Japanese era name|en|Japanese era name|WD=Q24706|lt=Japanese era|1=Japanese era name}} system).{{Cite book|title = Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794-1869|last = Ponsonby-Fane|first = Richard|year = 1956|pages = 154|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=9aLpAAAAMAAJ}} It was destroyed by fire in 1393, 1447, and 1467, rebuilt in 1597, and expanded in the Edo era.{{Cite book|title = Kyoto: a Cultural Guide|last = Martin|first = John and Phyllis|publisher = Tuttle Publishing|year = 2013|isbn = 9781462908172|pages = 177}} A large complex, it has varied over time between nine and twelve sub-temples. {{ill|Zenkei Shibayama|en|Zenkei Shibayama|WD=Q189630|lt=Zenkei Shibayama|1=Zenkei Shibayama}}, who provided a popular commentary on the [[:en:Mumonkan|Mumonkan]], was an abbot of the monastery. == Significance in Zen Buddhism == Nanzen-ji is not itself considered one of the "five great Zen temples of Kyoto"; however, it does play an important role in the "{{ill|Five Mountain System|en|Five Mountain System|WD=Q44372|lt=Five Mountain System|1=Five Mountain System}}" which was modified from Chinese roots. {{Nihongo|[[Tenryū-ji]]|天龍寺|''Tenryū-ji''}} is considered to be one of the so-called {{nihongo|{{ill|Five Mountain System|en|Five Mountain System|WD=Q44372|lt=Kyoto ''Gozan''|1=Five Mountain System}}|京都五山|''Kyōto gozan''}} or "five great Zen temples of Kyoto", along with {{nihongo|{{ill|Shōkoku-ji|en|Shōkoku-ji|WD=Q1786709|lt=Shōkoku-ji|1=Shōkoku-ji}}|相国寺|''Shōkoku-ji''}}, {{nihongo|[[Kennin-ji]]|建仁寺|''Kennin-ji''}}, {{nihongo|[[Tōfuku-ji]]|東福寺|''Tōfuku-ji''}}, and {{nihongo|{{ill|Manju-ji|en|Manju-ji|WD=Q3074317|lt=Manju-ji|1=Manju-ji}}|満寿寺|''Manju-ji''}}. The head temple presiding over the ''Gozan'' in Kyoto is Nanzen-ji.{{Cite book|title = The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Zen Buddhism|last = Baroni|first = Helen Josephine|publisher = The Rosen Publishing Group|year = 2002|isbn = 9780823922406|pages = 116|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=smNM4ElP3XgC}} After the completion of {{ill|Shōkoku-ji|en|Shōkoku-ji|WD=Q1786709|lt=Shōkoku-ji|1=Shōkoku-ji}} by {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|en|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|WD=Q248381|lt=Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|1=Ashikaga Yoshimitsu}} in 1386, a new Ranking system was created with Nanzen-ji at the top and in a class of its own. Nanzen-ji had the title of "First Temple of The Land" and played a supervising role.{{Cite book|title = Zen Buddhism: A History (Japan)|last = Dumoulin|first = Heinrich|publisher = World Wisdom, Inc.|year = 2005|isbn = 9780941532907|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=hfMkpD_Xr3sC}} {| class="wikitable" | colspan="3" align="center" | Nanzen-ji |- |  !Kyoto !Kamakura |- |First Rank |[[Tenryū-ji]] |[[Kenchō-ji]] |- |Second Rank |{{ill|Shōkoku-ji|en|Shōkoku-ji|WD=Q1786709|lt=Shōkoku-ji|1=Shōkoku-ji}} |[[Engaku-ji]] |- |Third Rank |[[Kennin-ji]] |{{ill|Jufuku-ji|en|Jufuku-ji|WD=Q167087|lt=Jufuku-ji|1=Jufuku-ji}} |- |Fourth Rank |[[Tōfuku-ji]] |{{ill|Jōchi-ji|en|Jōchi-ji|WD=Q1504539|lt=Jōchi-ji|1=Jōchi-ji}} |- |Fifth Rank |{{ill|Manju-ji|en|Manju-ji|WD=Q3074317|lt=Manju-ji|1=Manju-ji}} |[[:en:Jōmyō-ji (Kamakura)|Jōmyō-ji]] |- |} == Notable structures == === Sanmon === [[File:Nanzenji Sanmon 1.jpg|thumb|View from the second story of the Nanzen-ji Sanmon]] The temple's Sanmon gate was originally constructed in the 13th century, destroyed in 1369 at the order of the government, and reconstructed in 1628.{{Cite book|title = Daitokuji: The Visual Cultures of a Zen Monastery|last = Levine|first = Gregory P.A.|publisher = University of Washington Press|year = 2005|isbn = 9780295985404|pages = [https://archive.org/details/daitokujivisualc0000levi/page/94 94]|url = https://archive.org/details/daitokujivisualc0000levi|url-access = registration}} The gate contains stairs to an elevated viewing area, which was the setting for a famous scene in the 1778 {{ill|Kabuki|en|Kabuki|WD=Q199701|lt=Kabuki|1=Kabuki}} play {{ill|Sanmon Gosan no Kiri|en|Sanmon Gosan no Kiri|WD=Q11541534|lt=Sanmon Gosan no Kiri|1=Sanmon Gosan no Kiri}}{{Cite book|title = Historical Dictionary of Japanese Traditional Theatre|last = Leiter|first = Samuel L.|publisher = Rowman & Littlefield|year = 2014|isbn = 9781442239111|pages = 476|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=hCkvBQAAQBAJ}} inspired by the story of the criminal {{ill|Ishikawa Goemon|en|Ishikawa Goemon|WD=Q707279|lt=Ishikawa Goemon|1=Ishikawa Goemon}} who is said to have spoken of the beauty of the view (but who was executed prior to the construction of the current gate). === Hōjō === The hōjō (abbot's quarters) of Nanzen-ji is notable both for its gardens and its art. The garden of the hōjō is considered one of the most significant examples of {{ill|Japanese dry garden|en|Japanese dry garden|WD=Q244226|lt=karesansui gardens|1=Japanese dry garden}} (rock gardens), and was built in the 1600s by [[:en:Kobori Masakazu|Kobori Enshu]]. The garden mirrors natural forms, and is seventy percent gravel.{{Cite book|title = Art Of Japanese Gardens|last = Kuck|first = Loraine|publisher = Routledge|year = 2006|isbn = 9781136213625|pages = 230|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=DZ9FAAAAQBAJ}} It has been designated a national Place of Scenic Beauty. The hōjō itself, also known as the Seiryo-den, was given to the temple by the {{ill|Emperor Go-Yōzei|en|Emperor Go-Yōzei|WD=Q349440|lt=Emperor Go-Yōzei|1=Emperor Go-Yōzei}}.{{Cite book|title = The Temples of Kyoto|last = Richie|first = Donald|publisher = Tuttle Publishing|year = 2013|isbn = 9781462908578|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=ChTRAgAAQBAJ}} It contains a variety of important screen paintings on gold backgrounds, including two of tigers by {{ill|Kanō Tan'yū|en|Kanō Tan'yū|WD=Q961405|lt=Kanō Tan'yū|1=Kanō Tan'yū}}.{{Cite book|title = Of Gardens: Selected Essays|last = Deitz|first = Paula|publisher = University of Pennsylvania Press|year = 2011|isbn = 9780812206968|pages = 301|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=H9xzdCNIXvYC}} It has been designated a National Treasure. {{Gallery |File:Nanzen-ji_Ⅱ.jpg|The Hōjō |File:Nanzen-ji Ⅲ.jpg|The Hōjō garden |File:Hojo,_Konchi-in_-_IMG_5212.JPG|Crane paintings from inside the Hōjō}} === Nanzen-ji Aqueduct === Constructed in 1890 through the temple grounds to carry irrigation water from the {{ill|Lake Biwa Canal|en|Lake Biwa Canal|WD=Q2753954|lt=Lake Biwa Canal|1=Lake Biwa Canal}}. {{Gallery |File:Nanzenji aqueduct - IIMG 5411.JPG|Nanzen-ji aqueduct}} ==In media== Parts of the 2003 film ''{{ill|en:Lost in Translation (film)|en|en:Lost in Translation (film)|WD=Q107270|lt=Lost in Translation|1=Lost in Translation (film)}}'' were filmed there.{{cite web | url=https://www.imdb.com/title/tt0335266/locations/ | title=Lost in Translation (2003) - Filming & production - IMDb | website=[[IMDb]] }}{{cite web | url=https://tokyofox.net/2022/03/15/kyoto-filming-locations-lost-in-translation-2003/ | title=Kyoto Filming locations: Lost in Translation (2003) | date=15 March 2022 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/kyoto-voice/filming-locations-kyoto/ | title=Filming locations in Kyoto }}{{cite web | url=https://www.focusfeatures.com/article/film-locations_japan_lost-in-translation | title=Finding Japan in Lost in Translation }} == Gallery == {{Gallery |File:Kyoto Nanzenji06n3200.jpg|Hattō |File:Nanzen-in.JPG|Nanzen-in |File:Nanzenji_temple_-_IMG_5429.JPG|Small Hōjō garden |File:Zen_Garden,_Nanzen-ji_Temple_(7005735830)_(3).jpg|Another part of the complex |File:NanzenjiMomiji.jpg|In the fall }} == See also == *{{ill|Ishikawa Goemon|en|Ishikawa Goemon|WD=Q707279|lt=Ishikawa Goemon|1=Ishikawa Goemon}} * [[:en:List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto|List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] *{{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|WD=Q3245523|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)}} *{{ill|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)|en|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)|WD=Q7883225|lt=en:List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)}} ==References== {{Reflist|2}} ==Further reading== *{{cite book |last= Kawaguchi |first= Yoko |title= Japanese Zen Gardens |location= London |publisher= Francis Lincoln |date= 2014 |isbn= 978-0-7112-3447-5}} == External links == * {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category-inline|Nanzen-ji}} * 南禅寺 | native_name = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Kyoto_Nanzenji01s5s4272.jpg | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image_size = | alt = | caption = The [[sanmon]] | map_type = | map_size = | map_alt = | map_caption = | location = 86 Nanzenji Fukuchichō, {{ill|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Sakyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1321832|lt=Sakyō-ku|1=Sakyō-ku, Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}} | coordinates = {{coord|35|0|43.14|N|135|47|39.8|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | religious_affiliation = Nanzen-ji {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|WD=Q639625|lt=Rinzai school|1=Rinzai school}} | deity = [[:en:Shaka Nyorai|Shaka Nyorai]] (Shakyamuni Tathāgata) | country = Japan | functional_status = Head Temple | website = {{url|http://www.nanzen.net/english/index.html}} | founded_by = {{ill|Emperor Kameyama|en|Emperor Kameyama|WD=Q360972|lt=Emperor Kameyama|1=Emperor Kameyama}} and [[Mukan Fumon]] | year_completed = 1909 (Reconstruction) }} {{nihongo|'''Nanzen-ji'''|南禅寺|''Nanzen-ji''}}, or '''Zuiryusan Nanzen-ji''', formerly {{nihongo|'''Zenrin-ji'''|禅林寺|''Zenrin-ji''}}, is a {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|WD=Q7953|lt=Zen|1=Zen}} {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhist|1=Buddhism}} {{ill|Temple|en|Temple|WD=Q44539|lt=Temple|1=Temple}} in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. {{ill|Emperor Kameyama|en|Emperor Kameyama|WD=Q360972|lt=Emperor Kameyama|1=Emperor Kameyama}} established it in 1291 on the site of his previous detached palace. It is also the headquarters of the Nanzen-ji branch of [[:en:Rinzai Zen|Rinzai Zen]]. The precincts of Nanzen-ji are a {{ill|Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q858308|lt=nationally designated|1=Cultural Property (Japan)}} [[Monuments of Japan|Historic Site]] and the Hōjō gardens a [[Monuments of Japan|Place of Scenic Beauty]].{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/00003441 |title=南禅寺境内 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012}}{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/1705 |title=南禅寺方丈庭園 |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |access-date=10 February 2012}} The temple was destroyed in a fire in 1895 and rebuilt in 1909. ==History== Nanzen-ji was founded in the middle {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}} (1291, or {{ill|Shōō (Kamakura period)|en|Shōō (Kamakura period)|WD=Q1327371|lt=Shōō|1=Shōō (Kamakura period)}} 4 in the {{ill|Japanese era name|en|Japanese era name|WD=Q24706|lt=Japanese era|1=Japanese era name}} system).{{Cite book|title = Kyoto: The Old Capital of Japan, 794-1869|last = Ponsonby-Fane|first = Richard|year = 1956|pages = 154|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=9aLpAAAAMAAJ}} It was destroyed by fire in 1393, 1447, and 1467, rebuilt in 1597, and expanded in the Edo era.{{Cite book|title = Kyoto: a Cultural Guide|last = Martin|first = John and Phyllis|publisher = Tuttle Publishing|year = 2013|isbn = 9781462908172|pages = 177}} A large complex, it has varied over time between nine and twelve sub-temples. {{ill|Zenkei Shibayama|en|Zenkei Shibayama|WD=Q189630|lt=Zenkei Shibayama|1=Zenkei Shibayama}}, who provided a popular commentary on the [[:en:Mumonkan|Mumonkan]], was an abbot of the monastery. == Significance in Zen Buddhism == Nanzen-ji is not itself considered one of the "five great Zen temples of Kyoto"; however, it does play an important role in the "{{ill|Five Mountain System|en|Five Mountain System|WD=Q44372|lt=Five Mountain System|1=Five Mountain System}}" which was modified from Chinese roots. {{Nihongo|[[Tenryū-ji]]|天龍寺|''Tenryū-ji''}} is considered to be one of the so-called {{nihongo|{{ill|Five Mountain System|en|Five Mountain System|WD=Q44372|lt=Kyoto ''Gozan''|1=Five Mountain System}}|京都五山|''Kyōto gozan''}} or "five great Zen temples of Kyoto", along with {{nihongo|{{ill|Shōkoku-ji|en|Shōkoku-ji|WD=Q1786709|lt=Shōkoku-ji|1=Shōkoku-ji}}|相国寺|''Shōkoku-ji''}}, {{nihongo|[[Kennin-ji]]|建仁寺|''Kennin-ji''}}, {{nihongo|[[Tōfuku-ji]]|東福寺|''Tōfuku-ji''}}, and {{nihongo|{{ill|Manju-ji|en|Manju-ji|WD=Q3074317|lt=Manju-ji|1=Manju-ji}}|満寿寺|''Manju-ji''}}. The head temple presiding over the ''Gozan'' in Kyoto is Nanzen-ji.{{Cite book|title = The Illustrated Encyclopedia of Zen Buddhism|last = Baroni|first = Helen Josephine|publisher = The Rosen Publishing Group|year = 2002|isbn = 9780823922406|pages = 116|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=smNM4ElP3XgC}} After the completion of {{ill|Shōkoku-ji|en|Shōkoku-ji|WD=Q1786709|lt=Shōkoku-ji|1=Shōkoku-ji}} by {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|en|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|WD=Q248381|lt=Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|1=Ashikaga Yoshimitsu}} in 1386, a new Ranking system was created with Nanzen-ji at the top and in a class of its own. Nanzen-ji had the title of "First Temple of The Land" and played a supervising role.{{Cite book|title = Zen Buddhism: A History (Japan)|last = Dumoulin|first = Heinrich|publisher = World Wisdom, Inc.|year = 2005|isbn = 9780941532907|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=hfMkpD_Xr3sC}} {| class="wikitable" | colspan="3" align="center" | Nanzen-ji |- |  !Kyoto !Kamakura |- |First Rank |[[Tenryū-ji]] |[[Kenchō-ji]] |- |Second Rank |{{ill|Shōkoku-ji|en|Shōkoku-ji|WD=Q1786709|lt=Shōkoku-ji|1=Shōkoku-ji}} |[[Engaku-ji]] |- |Third Rank |[[Kennin-ji]] |{{ill|Jufuku-ji|en|Jufuku-ji|WD=Q167087|lt=Jufuku-ji|1=Jufuku-ji}} |- |Fourth Rank |[[Tōfuku-ji]] |{{ill|Jōchi-ji|en|Jōchi-ji|WD=Q1504539|lt=Jōchi-ji|1=Jōchi-ji}} |- |Fifth Rank |{{ill|Manju-ji|en|Manju-ji|WD=Q3074317|lt=Manju-ji|1=Manju-ji}} |[[:en:Jōmyō-ji (Kamakura)|Jōmyō-ji]] |- |} == Notable structures == === Sanmon === [[File:Nanzenji Sanmon 1.jpg|thumb|View from the second story of the Nanzen-ji Sanmon]] The temple's Sanmon gate was originally constructed in the 13th century, destroyed in 1369 at the order of the government, and reconstructed in 1628.{{Cite book|title = Daitokuji: The Visual Cultures of a Zen Monastery|last = Levine|first = Gregory P.A.|publisher = University of Washington Press|year = 2005|isbn = 9780295985404|pages = [https://archive.org/details/daitokujivisualc0000levi/page/94 94]|url = https://archive.org/details/daitokujivisualc0000levi|url-access = registration}} The gate contains stairs to an elevated viewing area, which was the setting for a famous scene in the 1778 {{ill|Kabuki|en|Kabuki|WD=Q199701|lt=Kabuki|1=Kabuki}} play {{ill|Sanmon Gosan no Kiri|en|Sanmon Gosan no Kiri|WD=Q11541534|lt=Sanmon Gosan no Kiri|1=Sanmon Gosan no Kiri}}{{Cite book|title = Historical Dictionary of Japanese Traditional Theatre|last = Leiter|first = Samuel L.|publisher = Rowman & Littlefield|year = 2014|isbn = 9781442239111|pages = 476|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=hCkvBQAAQBAJ}} inspired by the story of the criminal {{ill|Ishikawa Goemon|en|Ishikawa Goemon|WD=Q707279|lt=Ishikawa Goemon|1=Ishikawa Goemon}} who is said to have spoken of the beauty of the view (but who was executed prior to the construction of the current gate). === Hōjō === The hōjō (abbot's quarters) of Nanzen-ji is notable both for its gardens and its art. The garden of the hōjō is considered one of the most significant examples of {{ill|Japanese dry garden|en|Japanese dry garden|WD=Q244226|lt=karesansui gardens|1=Japanese dry garden}} (rock gardens), and was built in the 1600s by [[:en:Kobori Masakazu|Kobori Enshu]]. The garden mirrors natural forms, and is seventy percent gravel.{{Cite book|title = Art Of Japanese Gardens|last = Kuck|first = Loraine|publisher = Routledge|year = 2006|isbn = 9781136213625|pages = 230|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=DZ9FAAAAQBAJ}} It has been designated a national Place of Scenic Beauty. The hōjō itself, also known as the Seiryo-den, was given to the temple by the {{ill|Emperor Go-Yōzei|en|Emperor Go-Yōzei|WD=Q349440|lt=Emperor Go-Yōzei|1=Emperor Go-Yōzei}}.{{Cite book|title = The Temples of Kyoto|last = Richie|first = Donald|publisher = Tuttle Publishing|year = 2013|isbn = 9781462908578|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=ChTRAgAAQBAJ}} It contains a variety of important screen paintings on gold backgrounds, including two of tigers by {{ill|Kanō Tan'yū|en|Kanō Tan'yū|WD=Q961405|lt=Kanō Tan'yū|1=Kanō Tan'yū}}.{{Cite book|title = Of Gardens: Selected Essays|last = Deitz|first = Paula|publisher = University of Pennsylvania Press|year = 2011|isbn = 9780812206968|pages = 301|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=H9xzdCNIXvYC}} It has been designated a National Treasure. {{Gallery |File:Nanzen-ji_Ⅱ.jpg|The Hōjō |File:Nanzen-ji Ⅲ.jpg|The Hōjō garden |File:Hojo,_Konchi-in_-_IMG_5212.JPG|Crane paintings from inside the Hōjō}} === Nanzen-ji Aqueduct === Constructed in 1890 through the temple grounds to carry irrigation water from the {{ill|Lake Biwa Canal|en|Lake Biwa Canal|WD=Q2753954|lt=Lake Biwa Canal|1=Lake Biwa Canal}}. {{Gallery |File:Nanzenji aqueduct - IIMG 5411.JPG|Nanzen-ji aqueduct}} ==In media== Parts of the 2003 film ''{{ill|en:Lost in Translation (film)|en|en:Lost in Translation (film)|WD=Q107270|lt=Lost in Translation|1=Lost in Translation (film)}}'' were filmed there.{{cite web | url=https://www.imdb.com/title/tt0335266/locations/ | title=Lost in Translation (2003) - Filming & production - IMDb | website=[[IMDb]] }}{{cite web | url=https://tokyofox.net/2022/03/15/kyoto-filming-locations-lost-in-translation-2003/ | title=Kyoto Filming locations: Lost in Translation (2003) | date=15 March 2022 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/kyoto-voice/filming-locations-kyoto/ | title=Filming locations in Kyoto }}{{cite web | url=https://www.focusfeatures.com/article/film-locations_japan_lost-in-translation | title=Finding Japan in Lost in Translation }} == Gallery == {{Gallery |File:Kyoto Nanzenji06n3200.jpg|Hattō |File:Nanzen-in.JPG|Nanzen-in |File:Nanzenji_temple_-_IMG_5429.JPG|Small Hōjō garden |File:Zen_Garden,_Nanzen-ji_Temple_(7005735830)_(3).jpg|Another part of the complex |File:NanzenjiMomiji.jpg|In the fall }} == See also == *{{ill|Ishikawa Goemon|en|Ishikawa Goemon|WD=Q707279|lt=Ishikawa Goemon|1=Ishikawa Goemon}} * [[:en:List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto|List of Buddhist temples in Kyoto]] *{{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|WD=Q3245523|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)}} *{{ill|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)|en|en:List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)|WD=Q7883225|lt=en:List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (ancient documents)}} ==References== {{Reflist|2}} ==Further reading== *{{cite book |last= Kawaguchi |first= Yoko |title= Japanese Zen Gardens |location= London |publisher= Francis Lincoln |date= 2014 |isbn= 978-0-7112-3447-5}} == External links == * {{Commons Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 2]] [[Category-inline|Nanzen-ji}} * Rinzai and Obaku Zen]: [http://zen.rinnou.net/head_temples/03nanzen.html Nanzen-ji] * [http://www.sacred-destinations.com/japan/kyoto.htm Sacred Destinations]: It is also the headquarters of the Nanzen-ji branch of [[:en:Rinzai Zen|Rinzai Zen]]. The garden mirrors natural forms, and is seventy percent gravel.{{Cite book|title = Art Of Japanese Gardens|last = Kuck|first = Loraine|publisher = Routledge|year = 2006|isbn = 9781136213625|pages = 230|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=DZ9FAAAAQBAJ}} It has been designated a national Place of Scenic Beauty. The hōjō itself, also known as the Seiryo-den, was given to the temple by the {{ill|Emperor Go-Yōzei|en|Emperor Go-Yōzei|WD=Q349440|lt=Emperor Go-Yōzei|1=Emperor Go-Yōzei}}.{{Cite book|title = The Temples of Kyoto|last = Richie|first = Donald|publisher = Tuttle Publishing|year = 2013|isbn = 9781462908578|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=ChTRAgAAQBAJ}} It contains a variety of important screen paintings on gold backgrounds, including two of tigers by {{ill|Kanō Tan'yū|en|Kanō Tan'yū|WD=Q961405|lt=Kanō Tan'yū|1=Kanō Tan'yū}}.{{Cite book|title = Of Gardens: Selected Essays|last = Deitz|first = Paula|publisher = University of Pennsylvania Press|year = 2011|isbn = 9780812206968|pages = 301|url = https://books.google.com/books?id=H9xzdCNIXvYC}} It has been designated a National Treasure. {{Gallery |File:Nanzen-ji_Ⅱ.jpg|The Hōjō |File:Nanzen-ji Ⅲ.jpg|The Hōjō garden |File:Hojo,_Konchi-in_-_IMG_5212.JPG|Crane paintings from inside the Hōjō}} === Nanzen-ji Aqueduct === Constructed in 1890 through the temple grounds to carry irrigation water from the {{ill|Lake Biwa Canal|en|Lake Biwa Canal|WD=Q2753954|lt=Lake Biwa Canal|1=Lake Biwa Canal}}. {{Gallery |File:Nanzenji aqueduct - IIMG 5411.JPG|Nanzen-ji aqueduct}} ==In media== Parts of the 2003 film ''{{ill|en:Lost in Translation (film)|en|en:Lost in Translation (film)|WD=Q107270|lt=Lost in Translation|1=Lost in Translation (film)}}'' were filmed there.{{cite web | url=https://www.imdb.com/title/tt0335266/locations/ | title=Lost in Translation (2003) - Filming & production - IMDb | website=[[IMDb]] }}{{cite web | url=https://tokyofox.net/2022/03/15/kyoto-filming-locations-lost-in-translation-2003/ | title=Kyoto Filming locations: Lost in Translation (2003) | date=15 March 2022 }}{{cite web | url=https://www.discoverkyoto.com/kyoto-voice/filming-locations-kyoto/ | title=Filming locations in Kyoto }}{{cite web |
